_meta.purpose: Structured corpus of US state privacy enforcement actions, settlements, and notable adjacent matters. Each record is normalized so scripts/precedent_match.py can return analogous matters when given a gap.
_meta.scope: State comprehensive privacy laws (CCPA/CPRA, VCDPA, CPA, CTDPA, UCPA, TDPSA, OCPA, FDBR, ICDPA, INCDPA, MCDPA, MODPA, MN MCDPA, NDPA, KCDPA, NHDPA, NJDPA, RIDTPPA, TIPA, DPDPA-DE) plus closely-adjacent federal/state actions where the violation theory parallels state privacy law (FTC privacy cases; CIPA wiretap; BIPA biometric; HBNR; VPPA). Federal sectoral standalone (HIPAA OCR, GLBA, COPPA solo) excluded unless used as state-AG predicate.
_meta.limitations: This corpus is built from publicly-reported settlements and complaints. Settlement language often differs from the underlying allegations; the `violation_theories` capture pleaded theories, not adjudicated facts. Verify each entry against current sources before citing in client-facing work product. Settlement amounts sometimes include restitution and injunctive valuation in addition to civil penalties; the `monetary_amount_usd` field captures the headline figure as reported.

fields.case_name: Short caption commonly used in practice.
fields.year: Year of disposition (settlement date or judgment date).
fields.regulator: Enforcing entity. Multiple if joint action.
fields.statute_sections: Specific code or regulatory sections cited.
fields.violation_theories: The pleaded violation theories. Use the `tag` taxonomy below for matching.
fields.monetary_amount_usd: Headline settlement or penalty figure in USD. Null if injunctive-only.
fields.remediation_imposed: Material non-monetary remediation (audits, attestations, structural changes).
fields.citation: Where to find the source document.

actions.case_name: In re Sephora
actions.full_caption: People v. Sephora USA, Inc., No. CGC-22-601380 (Cal. Super. Ct., S.F. Cnty.)
actions.factual_pattern: Sephora exchanged consumer PI with third-party advertising and analytics partners in a manner the AG concluded constituted a 'sale' under CCPA, but failed to disclose the sale, omitted the 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link, and did not honor the Global Privacy Control. The AG also alleged the failure constituted an unfair business practice under the UCL.
remediation_imposed: Required to clearly disclose sale of PI
Provide DNS link
Process opt-outs received via GPC
2-year compliance reporting to AG
actions.citation: Cal. AG Press Release Aug. 24, 2022; Final Judgment & Stipulated Permanent Injunction.
operational_lessons: GPC handling is enforceable and the first published California enforcement action established that.
Data exchanges with adtech partners can constitute 'sale' even if no money changes hands directly.
UCL § 17200 is available as a parallel theory, expanding remedies.

actions.case_name: People v. DoorDash, Inc.
actions.full_caption: People v. DoorDash, Inc., No. CGC-24-612617 (Cal. Super. Ct., S.F. Cnty.)
actions.factual_pattern: DoorDash participated in marketing cooperatives that exchanged customer personal information with other businesses to obtain targeting data for new-customer acquisition. The AG concluded these exchanges constituted 'sales' under CCPA, but were not disclosed and consumers were not given a means to opt out.
remediation_imposed: Updated privacy disclosures
Inventory of data partners
Compliance reporting
actions.citation: Cal. AG Press Release Feb. 21, 2024; Final Judgment.
operational_lessons: Marketing data cooperatives — including bulk demographic exchanges with non-affiliated businesses — fall within CCPA's broad definition of 'sale.'
The AG continues to interpret 'sale' aggressively in the absence of monetary exchange.

actions.case_name: In re American Honda Motor Co. (CPPA)
actions.full_caption: In the Matter of American Honda Motor Co., Stipulated Final Order (CPPA 2024)
actions.factual_pattern: First public CPPA enforcement action (March 2025), following the agency's connected-vehicle investigative sweep announced July 2023. The CPPA's Order accounts for Honda's conduct toward 153 alleged violations: requiring excessive verification information for opt-out and limit-use requests in violation of CCPA Regs § 7060; opt-out methods that were not symmetrical with opt-in flows; processor contracts that did not contain the CCPA-required terms under § 7051; and inadequate disclosures regarding rights and authorized agents. The CPPA's findings are not specific to the connected-vehicle industry and apply to any CCPA-covered business.
remediation_imposed: Update rights-request processes
Conform processor contracts to § 7051
Train employees on CCPA rights handling
Compliance reporting to CPPA
actions.citation: CPPA Stipulated Final Order March 12, 2025.
operational_lessons: CPPA's first independent enforcement targeted operational mechanics, not headline-grabbing data sales — verification, opt-out symmetry, and processor contracts are scrutinized.
Asking for more identity-verification data than necessary IS a violation, not a defensive practice.
Opt-out path 'symmetry' (similar number of clicks / friction as opt-in path) is now an enforced expectation.

The 153-violation scope of the CPPA's order — relative to Honda selling/sharing PI of more than 100,000 California consumers annually — signals the agency will pursue any violation, even where the affected fraction of consumer interactions is small.

actions.case_name: In re Tilting Point Media
actions.full_caption: People v. Tilting Point Media LLC, No. CGC-24-... (Cal. Super. Ct., S.F. Cnty.)
actions.factual_pattern: Tilting Point operated a children's mobile game with default settings that shared minor users' data with third-party advertising SDKs without verifiable parental consent and without separate opt-in for sale/sharing of minor data as required under CCPA's heightened minor provisions.
remediation_imposed: Suppression of third-party SDKs in default child configurations
Audit of advertising integrations
Compliance reporting
actions.citation: Cal. AG Press Release Sept. 2024.
operational_lessons: CCPA's affirmative-consent requirement for sale/share of consumer data of known minors under 16 is independently enforceable — and overlaps with COPPA's parental-consent requirement.
Default-on third-party advertising SDK behavior in child-directed apps is a high-priority enforcement target.

actions.case_name: Texas v. Meta Platforms
actions.full_caption: State of Texas v. Meta Platforms, Inc., No. 22-0121 (Tex. Dist. Ct., Harrison Cnty.)
actions.factual_pattern: Texas alleged Meta's prior facial-recognition tagging features captured Texans' face geometry without informed consent. Suit predates and is technically separate from TDPSA, but reflects the AG's biometric-privacy posture and was filed and settled under the AG's pre-TDPSA framework.
remediation_imposed: Discontinuation of facial-recognition feature in Texas (already discontinued at time of settlement)
5-year compliance reporting
actions.citation: Tex. AG Press Release July 30, 2024; Settlement Agreement.
operational_lessons: Texas AG's willingness to pursue billion-dollar settlements under specialized privacy statutes is the dominant signal of TDPSA enforcement posture.
CUBI's written-consent requirement is strictly enforced; 'implied' consent does not satisfy the standard.
While CUBI is separate from TDPSA, the AG enforcement infrastructure is the same — a controller subject to TDPSA should expect the same enforcement intensity.

actions.case_name: Texas v. Google LLC
actions.full_caption: State of Texas v. Google LLC, No. 22-0237 (Tex. Dist. Ct., Harrison Cnty.)
actions.factual_pattern: Texas alleged Google collected facial-geometry, voiceprint, and geolocation data from Texas users via Photos, Assistant, and 'incognito' modes inconsistent with disclosed practices.
remediation_imposed: Disclosed practice changes
Compliance reporting
actions.citation: Tex. AG Press Release May 2024.
operational_lessons: Texas AG combines CUBI and DTPA theories to amplify damages exposure.
Representations about privacy-protective modes (e.g., incognito) are scrutinized for accuracy.

actions.case_name: Tex. AG TDPSA Investigations
actions.full_caption: Texas AG TDPSA initial enforcement initiative (broad investigatory demands)
actions.respondent: Multiple platforms (publicly named including TikTok, X, Discord)
statutes: Tex. Bus. & Com. Code §§ 541.001 et seq.
actions.factual_pattern: At and after TDPSA's July 1, 2024 effective date, the AG launched investigatory demand letters and announced an enforcement initiative focused on websites' privacy notice content, sensitive-data handling, and minor protections (interacting with the parallel SCOPE Act).
remediation_imposed: Investigations ongoing as of source date; some matters settled under seal
actions.citation: Tex. AG announcement June 2024.
operational_lessons: TDPSA enforcement began on day one — there is no ramp-up period in practice.
Notice-content adequacy and minor-data treatment are the AG's first-priority issues.
Texas AG has the largest active state privacy enforcement program in 2024-2025.

actions.case_name: United States v. GoodRx Holdings, Inc.
actions.full_caption: United States v. GoodRx Holdings, Inc., No. 3:23-cv-00460 (N.D. Cal.)
statutes: Health Breach Notification Rule (16 C.F.R. Part 318)
actions.factual_pattern: GoodRx deployed Meta and Google advertising pixels on health-related pages such that consumers' medication searches, prescriptions, and health conditions were transmitted to advertising partners along with persistent identifiers. The FTC alleged this violated GoodRx's representations and constituted an unauthorized disclosure under HBNR.
remediation_imposed: Permanent prohibition on sharing health data for advertising
Required disclosure of relationships with advertising partners
20-year compliance reporting
Required deletion of relevant data with advertising partners
actions.citation: FTC Press Release Feb. 1, 2023; Stipulated Order.
operational_lessons: Health-pixel deployments are a high-priority federal enforcement area; same theory available to state AGs under sensitive-data provisions of CO/CT/VA et al.
The FTC's first HBNR enforcement signaled that 'health information' is interpreted broadly to include URLs, prescription names, and conditions — not only formal medical records.
20-year reporting orders create compounding compliance burden — settlement headline figure understates total cost.

actions.case_name: In re BetterHelp, Inc.
actions.full_caption: In the Matter of BetterHelp, Inc., FTC File No. 202 3169
actions.factual_pattern: BetterHelp shared mental-health-questionnaire data and email addresses with advertising platforms (Facebook, Snapchat, Criteo, Pinterest) for retargeting purposes despite affirmative privacy promises that data would be kept confidential.
remediation_imposed: Restitution to consumers
Permanent prohibition on disclosure of health information for advertising
Deletion order to advertising partners
actions.citation: FTC Decision and Order July 14, 2023.
operational_lessons: Mental-health and telehealth data is treated as sensitive even where not technically PHI under HIPAA.
Affirmative privacy promises that the operator does not honor create § 5 deception liability independent of the underlying privacy law.
State sensitive-data definitions in CO/CT/VA/etc. encompass this type of data, opening parallel state liability.

actions.case_name: In re Avast Limited (revised entry)
actions.full_caption: In the Matter of Avast Ltd., FTC File No. 202 3033
actions.factual_pattern: Avast represented its products would block third-party tracking, while contemporaneously selling browsing-history data through subsidiary Jumpshot to enterprise customers, including ad networks. FTC also faulted Avast for failing to delete data when consumers uninstalled the product and for retention beyond stated purposes. Settlement: $16.5M plus structural remedies.
remediation_imposed: Permanent ban on sale of consumer browsing data for advertising
Notification to affected consumers
Deletion of data and any algorithms derived therefrom
Twenty-year compliance program
actions.citation: FTC Final Order April 2024.
operational_lessons: Sale of browsing-history data is a sensitive-data theory in most state privacy laws via 'precise information regarding online activity' or similar.
Affirmative anti-tracking representations create § 5 deception exposure when contradicted by upstream data flows.
Algorithm-deletion orders are now an emerging remedial trend — anything trained on tainted data may itself need to be destroyed.
Subsidiaries are not safe — Jumpshot's data sales were attributed to Avast as parent.

actions.case_name: In re Equifax (data broker registration)
actions.full_caption: Or. Dept. of Consumer & Bus. Servs. — Data Broker Registration Enforcement
regulator: Oregon Department of Consumer and Business Services
actions.respondent: Multiple unnamed data brokers (Oregon DCBS published initial registry)
statutes: Or. Rev. Stat. § 646A.504 (Oregon Data Broker Registry)
actions.factual_pattern: OR DCBS published its first Data Broker registry. Several entities operating data-broker-style activities had not registered. State sent compliance demand letters.
remediation_imposed: Registration; civil penalties for delayed registration
actions.citation: OR DCBS announcements 2024.
operational_lessons: Data broker registration is enforced separately from comprehensive privacy law — controllers that resell or license PD may be data brokers and miss this distinct registration obligation.
Other registration-required jurisdictions: California (CIPA-DBR), Texas (TX DPS data-broker registration), Vermont, Oregon.

actions.case_name: People v. Glow, Inc.
actions.full_caption: People v. Glow, Inc., No. CGC-20-585711 (Cal. Super. Ct., S.F. Cnty.)
actions.factual_pattern: Pre-CCPA action against fertility-tracking app for inadequate security controls that exposed sensitive reproductive-health data and for misleading privacy practices.
remediation_imposed: Required security program
Privacy assessments
Compliance reporting
actions.citation: Cal. AG Press Release Sept. 17, 2020.
operational_lessons: Reproductive-health data was treated as sensitive even pre-CCPA via § 1798.81.5; today this falls squarely within CCPA sensitive-PI category and most state sensitive-data definitions.
Foreshadowed the post-Dobbs heightened scrutiny on reproductive-health data.

actions.case_name: In re Blackbaud, Inc. — Multistate Settlement
actions.full_caption: Multistate AG Settlement with Blackbaud, Inc. (49 States + DC) (Oct. 5, 2023); In re Blackbaud, Inc., FTC File No. 2023181 (Feb. 1, 2024) (parallel)
actions.respondent_industry: Cloud software / nonprofit and education sector data processor
statutes: Various state consumer-protection and breach-notification statutes
actions.factual_pattern: Following the 2020 ransomware incident affecting Blackbaud customers' donor and student data, a multistate coalition of 49 state AGs plus the DC AG (excluding California) reached a $49.5 million settlement on October 5, 2023, led by Indiana and Vermont. The FTC pursued a parallel action announced February 1, 2024 (no monetary penalty), finalized May 2024, alleging deceptive security representations and unreasonable data retention. The hacker remained undetected in Blackbaud's network for three months in early 2020; Blackbaud paid a 24-Bitcoin (~$250k) ransom but did not verify deletion. Approximately 13,000 Blackbaud customer organizations and millions of downstream individuals were affected. Blackbaud was a processor for thousands of nonprofit, educational, and healthcare customers — making it one of the most consequential processor-side actions to date. The FTC order separately required deletion of unnecessary personal data.
remediation_imposed: Comprehensive information-security program
Mandatory third-party security assessments
Customer-notification reforms
FTC-mandated data deletion of unnecessary PI
Twenty-year FTC compliance reporting
actions.citation: Multistate AG Press Release Oct. 5, 2023; FTC Press Release Feb. 1, 2024 (proposed); FTC Final Order May 2024.
operational_lessons: Processors face direct AG exposure independent of flow-down through controllers — Blackbaud is the foundational case.
Multistate AG actions concentrate leverage; the 49-AG model produces settlement values 10x what a single state could obtain.
FTC and multistate AG enforcement can run in parallel — the same fact pattern can produce separate FTC consent decree + multistate AG settlement.
Data minimization is increasingly an enforced expectation: keeping data 'because we might need it' is increasingly characterized as unreasonable.

actions.case_name: Rivera et al. v. Google LLC (BIPA)
actions.full_caption: Rivera v. Google LLC, No. 1:16-cv-02714 (N.D. Ill.)
actions.factual_pattern: Google Photos' face-grouping feature collected face geometry from Illinois users without written informed consent or a publicly-disclosed retention schedule.
remediation_imposed: Distribution to class
Practice changes
actions.citation: Final Approval Order 2022.
operational_lessons: BIPA's $1k–$5k per-violation statutory damages plus 'per scan' interpretation in Cothron creates extraordinary class-action exposure independent of state comprehensive privacy law.
Any biometric processing — facial geometry from photos, voiceprints, fingerprint timekeeping — requires BIPA-compliant written consent and published retention schedule for IL users.

actions.case_name: Doe v. Meta Platforms (Pixel/Hospital wiretap)
actions.full_caption: Doe v. Meta Platforms, Inc., MDL No. 3037 (N.D. Cal.)
actions.factual_pattern: Multidistrict litigation alleging Meta Pixel embedded on hospital patient portals captured logged-in patient interactions (appointment requests, medication searches) and transmitted them to Meta, constituting wiretapping under CIPA and HIPAA breach.
remediation_imposed: Litigation ongoing
actions.citation: Doe v. Meta MDL filings.
operational_lessons: CIPA's two-party-consent statute is being used as a vehicle to challenge any logged-in tracking technology, not only call recording.
Pixels on healthcare portals are a high-volume class-action vector — combine BIPA-style statutory damages risk with HIPAA breach exposure.
Volume class-action trend: hundreds of CIPA filings per year as of 2023-2025.

actions.case_name: VPPA Class Actions (Multiple Defendants)
actions.full_caption: Various VPPA class actions including against NFL Enterprises, Paramount, NBC, etc.
actions.factual_pattern: Plaintiffs allege Meta Pixel deployed on video-streaming pages disclosed users' video-watching history along with Facebook IDs to Meta, constituting unauthorized disclosure of 'personally identifiable information' regarding video viewing.
remediation_imposed: Settlements vary; pixel removals
actions.citation: Various district court filings.
operational_lessons: Active high-volume plaintiff-bar-driven litigation area.
Companies offering streaming/video content with Meta/Google pixels face direct VPPA exposure separate from state privacy law.
Subscriber definition has been litigated — courts diverge on whether 'subscriber' requires payment.

actions.case_name: CT AG CTDPA Enforcement Report
actions.full_caption: Connecticut AG Report on CTDPA Enforcement
actions.factual_pattern: CT AG issued a public-facing report describing the most common violations encountered during compliance reviews and cure-period engagements: missing or inadequate privacy notices, missing opt-out methods, sensitive-data consent failures, and dark-pattern consent flows.
remediation_imposed: Cure-period remediation; sunset Jan 1, 2025
actions.citation: CT AG Report 2024.
operational_lessons: CT AG has provided a public roadmap of what gets cited during compliance reviews — controllers can prioritize specifically against this list.
Cure period sunset means future violations face direct penalty exposure.
Pattern of violations is consistent with what surfaces in CA and CO — same fixes apply across states.

actions.case_name: Florida v. TikTok / SCOPE Act-style Actions
actions.full_caption: Various — Florida AG enforcement under FDBR / minor-protection laws
actions.factual_pattern: Florida AG enforcement initiative targeting platforms over alleged failures regarding minor users — ad targeting to known minors, default-on personalization, and account-restriction tooling.
remediation_imposed: Investigation ongoing
actions.citation: Fla. AG Office press materials.
operational_lessons: FDBR's $50k-tripled-to-$150k-per-violation penalty for minor-related violations is the most severe per-violation in state privacy law.
Florida combines FDBR with parallel minor-protection statutes for compounded exposure.
Platforms below the $1B FDBR threshold may still be reachable via parallel minor-protection statutes.

actions.case_name: CO AG CPA Rulemaking and Enforcement Priorities
actions.full_caption: Colorado AG CPA Rulemaking — UOOM, Profiling, Loyalty (4 CCR 904-3)
actions.factual_pattern: CO AG has emphasized rulemaking as the primary enforcement vehicle, with the most prescriptive state privacy regulations in the country. The Universal Opt-Out Mechanism list is published and updated; controllers are required to honor any UOOM on the public list.
remediation_imposed: Compliance with rules; sunset of cure period Jan 1, 2025
actions.citation: 4 CCR § 904-3; CO AG UOOM list.
operational_lessons: Colorado's rules are the most prescriptive state privacy requirements; even controllers not subject to CPA often align to them as a multi-state baseline.
UOOM list is published — controllers must monitor for new UOOMs added to the list (currently includes GPC).
Loyalty program rules under § 6.04 are unusually specific and have no direct analog elsewhere.

actions.case_name: CPPA Connected Vehicle Enforcement Initiative
actions.full_caption: CPPA Investigation of Connected Vehicle Manufacturers
actions.respondent: Multiple connected-vehicle manufacturers (announced as a sweep)
actions.factual_pattern: CPPA announced a sweep of connected-vehicle data practices, including geolocation collection, in-cabin audio capture, sharing with insurers and data brokers, and inadequate disclosure to drivers and passengers.
remediation_imposed: Investigation ongoing
actions.citation: CPPA July 2023 announcement; ongoing investigations.
operational_lessons: Industry sweeps are an emerging CPPA enforcement model — entire sectors can come under simultaneous scrutiny.
Connected-vehicle sector has elevated sensitive-data exposure (precise geolocation, biometric, in-cabin audio).
Driver/passenger distinction matters: passengers are 'consumers' whose PI is processed but who never affirmatively engaged the controller.

actions.case_name: WA MHMDA Private Right of Action Filings
actions.full_caption: Various WA Wash. Rev. Code Ch. 19.373 (My Health My Data Act) actions
actions.factual_pattern: MHMDA's effective date in March 2024 (later for small business) triggered private right of action filings against health-adjacent operators — including pregnancy-tracking apps, mental-health apps, and tools collecting consumer-health-related browsing data.
remediation_imposed: Litigation ongoing
actions.citation: Various court filings 2024-2025.
operational_lessons: MHMDA is sectoral, not comprehensive — but its private right of action plus broad 'consumer health data' definition creates litigation pressure that often exceeds comprehensive-law exposure.
Operators of any service that infers health information (period trackers, fitness apps, sleep apps, wellness coaching) face MHMDA exposure regardless of HIPAA coverage status.
MHMDA-style approaches are being studied by other state legislatures.

actions.case_name: In re Todd Snyder, Inc. (CPPA)
actions.full_caption: In the Matter of Todd Snyder, Inc., CPPA Stipulated Final Order
actions.factual_pattern: CPPA's second standalone enforcement action. Todd Snyder's consent-management platform was misconfigured such that opt-out preference signals (including GPC) were not honored, the Do-Not-Sell-or-Share link did not function consistently, and the rights-request workflow required excessive identity verification. The agency emphasized that controllers using third-party CMP/privacy tooling must actively monitor and test the tooling — vendor deployment alone does not discharge the controller's obligation.
remediation_imposed: Reconfiguration of CMP and opt-out signal handling
Testing protocol for opt-out mechanisms
Annual compliance reports to CPPA
actions.citation: CPPA Stipulated Final Order May 6, 2025.
operational_lessons: Vendor-deployed CMPs are not an excuse — controllers must test that opt-out signals are actually honored end-to-end.
Misconfiguration is enforceable. The CPPA does not require evidence of intent.
Verification asymmetry (more friction to opt out than to opt in) is treated as a violation independent of the underlying signal handling.

actions.case_name: People v. Healthline Media LLC
actions.full_caption: People v. Healthline Media LLC (Cal. Super. Ct. 2025)
actions.factual_pattern: Largest CCPA settlement to date as of disposition. AG investigation found that even after consumers opted out via the DNS link, GPC, and the cookie banner ('triple opt-out'), 118 third-party advertising cookies continued firing and Healthline continued transmitting unique identifiers and article titles to advertising partners. Article titles included diagnosis-implying language such as 'You've Been Newly Diagnosed with MS,' which the AG characterized as inferred sensitive personal information. The AG also found that Healthline's contracts with adtech vendors lacked CCPA § 7051 required terms — Healthline relied on industry-framework membership without verifying actual contract presence. First U.S. regulatory privacy enforcement action to fine a company for disclosing inferred sensitive PI rather than explicit health data.
remediation_imposed: Permanent injunction prohibiting sale/share of article-title combinations that reveal a diagnosed medical condition
Implementation of CCPA compliance program with opt-out testing
Annual review of all third-party contracts for CCPA-mandated terms
Three years of annual reports to AG on opt-out processing problems and remediation
actions.citation: Cal. AG Press Release July 1, 2025; Stipulated Final Judgment.
operational_lessons: Inferred sensitive data is sensitive data. Article titles, browsing patterns, and search inputs that allow inference of a diagnosis fall within the sensitive PI definition even though the data is not formally a medical record.
Industry-framework membership (e.g., IAB TCF) is not a substitute for CCPA-compliant contract language with each individual third party. Verify each contract.

CCPA's purpose-limitation principle (§ 1798.100(c)) is independently enforceable. Disclosure of data for 'unexpected uses' (third-party inferences, targeted advertising not aligned with collection purpose) is a separate violation distinct from sale/share.
GPC failure where the underlying CMP is misconfigured is a direct violation; the AG tested the deployment.
Healthcare-adjacent operators that disclaim HIPAA coverage are not safe — state sensitive-data definitions catch what HIPAA misses.

actions.case_name: In re Tractor Supply Co. (CPPA)
actions.full_caption: In the Matter of Tractor Supply Company, CPPA Stipulated Final Order
actions.factual_pattern: Largest CPPA penalty to date ($1.35M, September 2025). Tractor Supply failed to notify California consumers and job applicants of their privacy rights, lacked CCPA-required contractual privacy protections in agreements with adtech and other service providers, and provided opt-out mechanisms that did not function as required. First published CCPA enforcement action to address applicant/HR data — a reminder that California is the unique outlier in covering employee, applicant, and B2B data. Investigation triggered by a single consumer complaint received in early 2024.
remediation_imposed: Comprehensive overhaul of privacy disclosures including separate disclosures to job applicants and current/former employees
Updated service-provider and contractor agreements with full § 7051 terms
Implementation of working opt-out mechanisms
Annual certifications of compliance to CPPA
Tractor Supply acknowledged the CPPA's authority to investigate conduct predating the agency's July 2023 enforcement authority
actions.citation: CPPA Stipulated Final Order September 30, 2025.
operational_lessons: California's CCPA covers job applicants, current employees, and former employees. Privacy notices and rights-fulfillment must address them as 'consumers.' This is the first published action to address it directly.
A single consumer complaint can trigger a multi-million-dollar enforcement. Volume of complaints does not control investigation triggers.
Vendor agreements must contain § 7051 terms expressly. CPPA Enforcement Chief Macko: 'we will look broadly across industries to identify violations.'
The CPPA asserts investigatory authority over conduct predating its July 2023 enforcement date when the conduct violates the CCPA's longer-standing requirements.

actions.case_name: In re Background Alert, Inc. (CPPA Delete Act)
actions.full_caption: In the Matter of Background Alert, Inc., CPPA Stipulated Final Order
statutes: Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.99.80 et seq. (Delete Act)
actions.factual_pattern: Part of the CPPA's data-broker registration sweep announced October 30, 2024. Background Alert amassed billions of public records and drew inferences from those records to create consumer profiles, which it sold via backgroundalert.com. The company failed to register or pay the annual fee under the Delete Act. Settlement requires Background Alert to shut down operations through 2028 or pay a $50,000 fine.
remediation_imposed: Operational shutdown through 2028 (alternative to monetary penalty)
Permanent injunction against operating as an unregistered data broker in CA
actions.citation: CPPA Stipulated Final Order February 27, 2025.
operational_lessons: Delete Act registration is enforceable independently of CCPA applicability. A controller that draws inferences from public records and sells the resulting profiles is a data broker.
CPPA Enforcement Chief Macko: 'we won't hesitate to pursue violations based on inferences and profiling.' Inference-based products are within scope.

California's Delete Act introduces DROP (Data Removal Operation Platform), a single-request mechanism that propagates deletion across registered brokers. Registered status is now business-critical for any inference-driven data product.

actions.case_name: CPPA Data Broker Enforcement Strike Force (KMA, ROR Partners, Datamasters, S&P Global, others)
actions.full_caption: Various CPPA Stipulated Final Orders — Data Broker Registration Enforcement
actions.respondent: Multiple — KMA ($55,800); ROR Partners ($56,600); Rickenbacher Data/Datamasters ($45,000); S&P Global ($62,600); Jerico Pictures/National Public Data; others
statutes: Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.99.80 et seq. (Delete Act)
actions.factual_pattern: Continuing sweep launched October 2024 by the CPPA Enforcement Division. Six-figure-fine pattern across multiple respondents for failure to register and pay the annual fee. Datamasters additionally faced an order to stop selling all Californians' personal information for selling lists segmented by health condition (Alzheimer's, drug addiction, bladder incontinence), perceived race, age, political views, and other sensitive characteristics. S&P Global's failure was characterized as an 'administrative error' but the agency still imposed a six-figure fine — registration is strict-liability.
remediation_imposed: Registration; payment of past-due fees and penalties
For Datamasters: permanent prohibition on selling Californians' data
actions.citation: CPPA Press Releases Jan-Dec 2025 and Jan 2026.
operational_lessons: Six published enforcement actions in 12 months establish that the data-broker sweep is sustained.
Any business that processes data of more than a threshold number of Californians from sources other than direct collection must evaluate broker status.
Sale of audience segments based on health, race, religion, or political views is a high-priority enforcement target — the segments themselves invite enforcement irrespective of registration status.
Administrative-error defenses are not credited; registration is a strict-liability obligation.

actions.case_name: Texas v. Allstate Corp. and Arity (TDPSA / Data Broker / Insurance Code)
actions.full_caption: State of Texas v. Allstate Corp. et al. (Tex. Dist. Ct., Montgomery Cnty. 2025)
actions.respondent: Allstate Corp. and five subsidiaries (Arity, Arity 875, Arity Services)
statutes: Tex. Bus. & Com. Code §§ 541.001 et seq. (TDPSA)
Tex. Bus. & Com. Code Ch. 109 (Data Broker Law)
actions.factual_pattern: First-ever lawsuit by any state AG enforcing a comprehensive state privacy law (filed January 13, 2025). Allstate's Arity subsidiary developed an SDK licensed to consumer apps (Life360, GasBuddy, Fuel Rewards, Routely) that collected precise geolocation and driving-behavior data from over 45 million users. Arity then sold this data to insurers — including Allstate — that used it to set premiums, deny coverage, or non-renew policies. The privacy notices for the third-party host apps did not disclose Arity's collection or sale of sensitive data; Arity did not register as a data broker in Texas. AG issued a 30-day cure notice November 29, 2024; defendants did not cure; AG filed suit. Theories include TDPSA (notice inadequacy, sensitive-data consent failure, sale-disclosure failure, opt-out absence), Data Broker Law (registration failure), and DTPA (deceptive notices). State claims relief in excess of $1 million.
remediation_imposed: Litigation pending as of disposition date

AG seeks: $7,500/violation TDPSA penalty; $10,000 daily Data Broker Law penalty (capped at $10,000/12 months); Insurance Code penalties; deletion of all data including data with downstream third parties; restitution
actions.citation: Tex. AG Press Release Jan 13, 2025; Complaint, Tex. v. Allstate, Cause No. 24-12-19386 (Tex. Dist. Ct., Montgomery Cnty.).
operational_lessons: Texas AG litigates rather than settles privately. The cure period was used; the cure response was inadequate; the AG filed suit. Other states' AGs typically settle quietly. Texas is the outlier and the most aggressive enforcer.
SDK-driven data collection is a growing focus area. The host app's privacy notice does not cure the SDK provider's separate notice obligation. Both have notice and consent obligations under TDPSA.
Multi-statute compounding: TDPSA + Data Broker Law + Insurance Code + DTPA produces aggregate exposure far exceeding any single law's penalty cap.
TDPSA's required disclosure 'NOTICE: We may sell your sensitive personal data' is a strict-liability requirement when applicable; absence is per-se violation.

Connected-vehicle data (precise geolocation + driving behavior) is treated as sensitive even where the underlying statute does not enumerate it — the AG framed precise geolocation as sensitive under TDPSA § 541.001(31).

actions.case_name: Texas v. General Motors / OnStar (Connected-vehicle data)
actions.full_caption: State of Texas v. General Motors LLC, OnStar LLC (Tex. Dist. Ct. 2024)
actions.factual_pattern: Texas AG sued GM in August 2024 alleging that GM and OnStar collected driving-behavior data (acceleration, hard braking, speed) and precise geolocation from over 1.8 million Texas drivers via the Smart Driver feature, then sold the data to consumer reporting agencies (LexisNexis Risk Solutions and Verisk) which in turn sold to insurers. GM's enrollment process did not adequately disclose the data sale. Action predates TDPSA but illustrates AG's connected-vehicle focus; follow-up investigations expanded to Ford, Hyundai, Toyota, Fiat Chrysler in early 2025.
remediation_imposed: Litigation pending; FTC parallel action with similar facts settled with 5-year sale ban + 20-year consent requirement
actions.citation: Tex. AG Press Release Aug. 2024.
operational_lessons: Connected-vehicle data is a multi-jurisdictional focus area: TX AG, CA CPPA (industry sweep), FTC (settled with GM January 2026), Oregon (auto-manufacturer carve-in to OCPA effective September 2025).
Even without comprehensive privacy law applicability, DTPA-style consumer-protection theories reach connected-vehicle deployments.
Vendor relationships (LexisNexis, Verisk as consumer reporting agencies) create FCRA-adjacent exposure as well.

actions.case_name: Texas TDPSA Cure-Letter Sweep (100+ companies)
actions.full_caption: Various — Texas AG TDPSA cure-letter campaign and Data Broker Law non-registration notices
actions.respondent_industry: Various — including data brokers, social media, AI, automakers
statutes: Tex. Bus. & Com. Code §§ 541.001 et seq.
actions.factual_pattern: TDPSA cure-letter campaign post July 1, 2024 effective date. AG sent letters to over 100 companies notifying them of failure to register as data brokers under the Texas Data Broker Law. May 6, 2025: AG sent noncompliance notices to several Chinese-owned companies under TDPSA for data-processing and opt-out failures. Most matters resolved through the 30-day cure process. Volume signals that AG is not waiting for complaints — proactive sweeps are the operating model.
remediation_imposed: Cure-period remediation; some matters escalated
actions.citation: Tex. AG Press Releases throughout 2024-2025.
operational_lessons: Texas AG enforcement is proactive and sweep-based, not complaint-driven. Controllers should not rely on under-the-radar status.
100+ companies received cure letters in 2024 alone. Most have remediated; the visibility of the program is itself a deterrent.
The 30-day cure period is in effect (no sunset under TDPSA) — but cure responses must be thorough; Allstate's inadequate cure response triggered the first lawsuit.
Foreign-ownership focus: AG specifically targeted Chinese-owned companies in May 2025, signaling national-security-adjacent enforcement.

actions.case_name: Connecticut v. TicketNetwork, Inc. (CTDPA — first monetary settlement)
actions.full_caption: Connecticut AG Settlement with TicketNetwork, Inc.
actions.factual_pattern: First monetary penalty under the CTDPA. AG issued cure notice November 9, 2023 flagging that TicketNetwork's privacy notice was 'largely unreadable, missing key data rights, and contained rights mechanisms that were misconfigured or inoperable.' Cure period expired January 8, 2024. TicketNetwork did not cure. AG noted that across four privacy-notice sweeps and 24+ cure notices, TicketNetwork was 'the only entity that repeatedly represented that they had resolved deficiencies when they had not done so.' Cure period sunset January 1, 2025; AG has since begun issuing Notices of Violation directly. Settlement: $85,000 plus injunctive compliance program, metrics reporting to AG, and ongoing monitoring.
remediation_imposed: Full CTDPA compliance
Quarterly reporting of consumer rights request metrics to AG
Privacy notice rewrite
Functional consumer rights mechanisms (verified by AG testing)
actions.citation: CT AG Press Release July 8, 2025.
operational_lessons: The CTDPA cure period sunset January 1, 2025. Future violations face direct penalty exposure with no statutory cushion.
Tong's office tested rights mechanisms — 'misconfigured or inoperable' findings indicate the AG ran the workflows.
Misrepresenting cure compliance is itself an aggravating factor. Honest engagement with AG cure letters is essential.
CTDPA was substantially amended by Public Act 25-113 (June 2025), with new provisions effective July 1, 2026 — including a lower applicability threshold of 35,000 CT residents.
Privacy-notice deficiencies are the most common violation pattern in CT enforcement (per AG report).

actions.case_name: Oregon OCPA Cure-Period Enforcement (21+ matters)
actions.full_caption: Various Oregon DOJ OCPA cure letters and resolutions
actions.respondent_industry: Various — including data brokers, retail, healthcare-adjacent
actions.factual_pattern: OCPA effective July 1, 2024 (for-profits) and July 1, 2025 (non-profits). DOJ issued 21+ cure letters in the first six months; DOJ Privacy Unit received 214 consumer complaints in the first year. Common deficiencies: (1) failure to offer the OCPA-unique right to know specific third parties (vendor names) to whom data was disclosed — OR is the first state to require this; (2) consumer rights responses limited to consumer-facing data only, omitting back-end profiles, marketing inferences, and shopping patterns; (3) inaccessible or non-functional rights-request forms; (4) 'light' cure letters to data brokers with public-facing notices that did not address OCPA. Cure period sunset January 1, 2026; AG can now proceed directly to enforcement.
remediation_imposed: Privacy notice updates to disclose specific third parties
Rights-request workflow expansion to include back-end data
Closed cure-letter matters (all 21 in first 6 months were closed in cure)
actions.citation: OR DOJ OCPA Six-Month Enforcement Report (Mar 2025); One-Year Enforcement Report (Aug 2025).
operational_lessons: OR's specific-third-parties disclosure is operationally distinctive: a controller that lists categories only has not satisfied OCPA. Maintain a current vendor list keyed to consumer data flows for OR responses.
Back-end data (marketing profiles, shopping patterns, derived inferences) is responsive to OCPA rights requests. Do not limit responses to consumer-facing account data.
Auto-manufacturer carve-in: as of September 26, 2025, OCPA applies to all auto manufacturers regardless of consumer count.

Effective January 1, 2026: ban on selling precise geolocation; ban on selling data of children under 16; ban on targeted advertising and certain profiling for under-16 data; UOOM recognition mandate; cure period sunset.
OR's DOJ has built a public complaint pipeline — 214 complaints in year one signals high consumer engagement and investigation throughput.

actions.case_name: In re X-Mode Social, Inc. / Outlogic, LLC
actions.full_caption: In the Matter of X-Mode Social, Inc., Outlogic, LLC, FTC File No. 2023038
actions.factual_pattern: First FTC settlement banning a data broker from selling raw precise location data. X-Mode/Outlogic sold raw location data until May 2023 without removing visits to sensitive locations (reproductive health clinics, places of worship, domestic abuse shelters). The order requires X-Mode to develop and maintain a comprehensive list of sensitive locations and ensure no sharing/sale of location data tied to such locations.
remediation_imposed: Permanent ban on sale or sharing of sensitive location data
Mandatory sensitive-location list maintained by the broker
Notification to data recipients to delete data and any inferences derived from it
actions.citation: FTC Final Order April 11, 2024.
operational_lessons: Location data tied to sensitive locations is treated as sensitive personal information regardless of whether it includes any traditional health or PI identifier.
Brokers cannot satisfy compliance through general 'consumer-consent' representations passed up the chain — they must verify consent at the source and protect against downstream re-sale.
State sensitive-data definitions in CO, CT, VA, OR, MN, MD encompass precise geolocation and the X-Mode theory is exportable to state AG enforcement.

actions.case_name: In re Mobilewalla, Inc.; In re Gravy Analytics / Venntel
actions.full_caption: In the Matter of Mobilewalla, Inc., FTC File No. 202-3196; In the Matter of Gravy Analytics, Inc. and Venntel, Inc.
actions.respondent: Mobilewalla, Inc.; Gravy Analytics, Inc.; Venntel, Inc.
actions.respondent_industry: Data brokerage — location data and audience segments
actions.factual_pattern: Two parallel actions announced December 3, 2024. Mobilewalla collected over 500 million unique advertising identifiers paired with precise location data, including data captured from real-time bidding (RTB) exchanges even when Mobilewalla did not win the bid. FTC's first-ever prohibition on RTB-data harvesting for purposes other than participating in the auction. Mobilewalla also built audience segments by sensitive characteristic (medical conditions, religious beliefs, political activities) derived from location data. Gravy Analytics/Venntel collected location data from third-party suppliers and sold it to private and public sector customers, including federal agencies, while continuing to collect even after learning consumers had not consented.
remediation_imposed: Mobilewalla: ban on collecting RTB-bid data for purposes other than the auction; ban on sale of sensitive location data; deletion of historical data
Gravy/Venntel: ban on sale of sensitive location data; restrictions on collection where consent is not verified
actions.citation: FTC Press Releases Dec 3, 2024.
operational_lessons: Real-time bidding exchanges as a data source are now a recognized FTC enforcement target. Adtech that collects via RTB beyond the immediate auction is per-se unfair under § 5.

Audience segmentation by inferred sensitive characteristics (medical condition, religion, political activity) is unfair regardless of whether the underlying location data was 'public' or 'commercially available.'
Indefinite retention of raw location data is a separate violation from collection and sale.
The FTC's location-data line of cases — Kochava (2022), X-Mode (2024), InMarket (2024), Mobilewalla (2024), Gravy (2024) — establishes a coherent doctrinal framework state AGs are likely to import.

actions.case_name: United States v. Cerebral, Inc.
actions.full_caption: United States v. Cerebral, Inc. (FTC / DOJ enforcement)
actions.respondent_industry: Telehealth — mental health and substance use
statutes: Opioid Addiction Recovery Fraud Prevention Act (OARFPA)
actions.factual_pattern: Cerebral disclosed sensitive health data of approximately 3.2 million consumers — including contact information, medical and prescription histories, insurance details, religious beliefs, and sexual orientation — to LinkedIn, Snapchat, TikTok, and other advertising platforms via embedded tracking technologies, while promising consumers their data would receive 'safe, secure, and discreet' treatment. Additional violations: failure to revoke former-employee access; mailing 6,000+ promotional postcards that revealed treatment status; single-key Dropbox access for staff and contractors; multi-step deceptive cancellation process.
remediation_imposed: Permanent prohibition on disclosure of personal/health information for marketing or advertising (first-of-its-kind restriction)
Comprehensive privacy and security program with biennial third-party audits
Restitution to consumers for deceptive cancellation practices
20-year compliance reporting
actions.citation: FTC Press Release April 15, 2024; Stipulated Order, U.S. v. Cerebral, Inc., No. 1:24-cv-... (D.D.C.).
operational_lessons: Mental-health and substance-use data is treated as among the most sensitive categories. Permanent prohibitions (rather than restrictions) on advertising-related use are an emerging remedial model.
Operational security failures (former-employee access, postcards revealing diagnosis, shared cloud-storage credentials) compound privacy-promise violations into substantially larger settlements.

Telehealth and mental-health-adjacent operators must distinguish between their service-delivery purposes (which may permit data use) and advertising/marketing (which generally requires opt-in consent and may be flatly prohibited for sensitive PI).
Founder/CEO personal liability is on the table — the FTC named Robertson personally and that aspect of the case continues against him separately.

actions.case_name: In re Monument, Inc.
actions.full_caption: In the Matter of Monument, Inc., FTC File No. 2323002
actions.factual_pattern: Monument operated a telehealth platform treating alcohol use disorder. The FTC alleged Monument shared sensitive health information — including responses to intake questionnaires regarding alcohol consumption — with Meta, Google, and other advertising platforms via tracking pixels, despite affirmative representations that data would be kept confidential and HIPAA-compliant. Companion to the Cerebral and BetterHelp matters in the FTC's telehealth-tracking enforcement line.
remediation_imposed: Permanent restrictions on disclosure of health information for advertising
Notification to advertising partners to delete affected data
Comprehensive privacy program
actions.citation: FTC Press Release April 11, 2024.
operational_lessons: FTC's telehealth-pixel enforcement line — GoodRx (2023), BetterHelp (2023), Cerebral (2024), Monument (2024) — is uniform: any sensitive health data shared with advertising platforms via pixels is presumptively unlawful absent specific opt-in consent.

Affirmative HIPAA-compliance representations are scrutinized when actual practices contradict them. 'HIPAA-compliant' as a marketing claim creates § 5 deception liability when the operator is not actually a covered entity or business associate.
Intake questionnaire data is treated as sensitive health data the moment the consumer responds — not only after a formal diagnosis.

actions.case_name: In re General Motors LLC and OnStar LLC (FTC)
actions.full_caption: In the Matter of General Motors LLC, OnStar LLC, FTC File No. 2323086
actions.respondent: General Motors LLC; OnStar LLC; General Motors Holdings LLC
actions.factual_pattern: Final order entered January 14, 2026 (proposed January 2025) resolving allegations that GM and OnStar collected precise geolocation and driving-behavior data (including data sampled as frequently as every three seconds) from millions of vehicles via the OnStar Smart Driver feature and sold it to data brokers (LexisNexis Risk Solutions and Verisk), which sold it to insurers — affecting consumer insurance rates without consumer knowledge. FTC found GM used a misleading enrollment process that did not adequately disclose the data sale. GM had already discontinued Smart Driver in April 2024 following NYT reporting; the FTC nonetheless imposed structural remedies. First FTC action targeting connected vehicle data.
remediation_imposed: Five-year ban on disclosing geolocation and driver-behavior data to consumer reporting agencies
Twenty-year requirement to obtain affirmative express consent before collecting, using, or sharing connected vehicle data
Consumer right to obtain copies of and delete their data
Consumer right to disable precise geolocation collection where vehicle technology supports it
Twenty-year compliance reporting
actions.citation: FTC Final Order Jan 14, 2026; Press Release Jan 16, 2025 (proposed); Press Release Jan 14, 2026 (finalized).
operational_lessons: 20-year affirmative-consent requirements are the FTC's emerging structural remedy for sensitive-data violations. The compounding compliance cost over the order's term substantially exceeds any monetary settlement.
Connected-vehicle data is unequivocally treated as sensitive (precise geolocation + driving behavior) by the FTC, regardless of whether the data goes through a consumer reporting agency intermediary.
Discontinuing the program before settlement does not eliminate enforcement exposure — the FTC pursued GM despite the April 2024 program termination.
Affirmative consent must be 'clearly presented' and 'cannot be implied through silence or preselected options' — a direct rejection of dark-pattern consent flows in vehicle-purchase contexts.
The Texas AG case against GM (filed August 2024) is parallel to this FTC action; controllers in connected-vehicle space face overlapping federal and state enforcement.

actions.case_name: FTC v. Kochava Inc.
actions.full_caption: FTC v. Kochava Inc., No. 2:22-cv-00377 (D. Idaho)
actions.factual_pattern: FTC's foundational location-data case (filed 2022; amended complaint sustained 2024). Kochava sold mobile geolocation data linked to specific mobile advertising IDs (MAIDs), including data revealing visits to reproductive health clinics, addiction treatment facilities, places of worship, and domestic violence shelters. The 2024 ruling on the motion to dismiss the amended complaint allowed the FTC's unfairness theory to proceed and established the framework for subsequent location-data cases.
remediation_imposed: Litigation pending
actions.citation: FTC v. Kochava, No. 2:22-cv-00377 (D. Idaho); FTC Press Releases 2022-2024.
operational_lessons: Kochava is the doctrinal foundation for the FTC's location-data line. The 2024 ruling on motion to dismiss kept the unfairness theory alive and authorized the broader sweep that produced X-Mode, InMarket, Mobilewalla, and Gravy.
Mobile advertising identifiers (MAIDs) tied to precise geolocation are treated as personal data and the combination is sensitive when paired with sensitive-location visits.
FTC's 'substantial injury' analysis for unfairness focuses on aggregate consumer harm potential (stalking, discrimination, physical safety) rather than individualized consumer-by-consumer harm.

actions.case_name: Markels v. AARP (VPPA)
actions.full_caption: Markels et al. v. AARP, No. 4:22-cv-05499-YGR (N.D. Cal.)
actions.factual_pattern: Class settlement against AARP for use of the Meta Pixel on aarp.org pages with video content, allegedly disclosing members' video-viewing activity along with Facebook IDs to Meta without lawful consent. $12.5 million settlement fund — among the larger VPPA settlements.
remediation_imposed: Pixel suppression on portions of website where the URL identifies specific video content viewed
Reservation of ability to use Pixel where VPPA-compliant consent is obtained or where transmitted information does not identify specific videos
actions.citation: Markels v. AARP, settlement approved 2025.
operational_lessons: VPPA class settlements have ranged from low six figures (Springer Nature/Scientific American $900k, Limited Run Games $2.7M) to high seven and eight figures (AARP $12.5M, Forbes $7.5M, Boston Globe Media $5M).
Settlement structure typically combines monetary class fund with injunctive limitation on Pixel deployment for video pages — but reservation of consent-based use preserves operational flexibility.
Membership organizations and content publishers with large logged-in user bases are highest-exposure VPPA defendants.

actions.case_name: Solomon v. Flipps Media, Inc. (VPPA pixel ruling)
actions.full_caption: Solomon v. Flipps Media, Inc., No. 24-1199 (2d Cir. May 1, 2025)
actions.respondent: Flipps Media, Inc. (d/b/a Fite, Fite TV)
actions.factual_pattern: Second Circuit affirmed dismissal of VPPA class action premised on Meta Pixel transmissions, holding that the strings of code transmitted by the Pixel do not constitute 'personally identifiable information' under VPPA because the information would not 'readily permit an ordinary person to identify a specific individual's video-watching behavior.' En banc review denied July 28, 2025. The court itself noted that the decision 'effectively shut the door for Pixel-based VPPA claims' in the Second Circuit. Companion case (Hughes v. NFL) reinforced the holding. Sixth Circuit (Salazar v. Paramount Global) has reached a contrary expansive reading; circuit split is active.
remediation_imposed: Defense judgment
actions.citation: Solomon v. Flipps Media, Inc., No. 24-1199 (2d Cir. May 1, 2025); en banc denial July 28, 2025.
operational_lessons: VPPA defenses based on the 'ordinary person' standard are now meaningfully strengthened in the Second Circuit, Third Circuit, and Ninth Circuit.
The Sixth Circuit's broader 'reasonable foreseeability' standard creates a meaningful circuit split that will likely require Supreme Court resolution.
Defendants should evaluate forum and circuit-of-application carefully; pre-Solomon strategic settlements may have been overpriced.

Salazar v. NBA (2d Cir.) had earlier given an expansive reading; Solomon and Hughes refined and narrowed Salazar's reach by restricting it to scenarios where PII is transmitted in human-readable form rather than as opaque code.
VPPA continues to apply to genuinely-PII transmissions; the Solomon defense depends on the technical specifics of the Pixel deployment.

actions.case_name: Lee v. Springer Nature America, Inc. (Scientific American)
actions.full_caption: Lee v. Springer Nature America, Inc., No. 24-cv-4493 (S.D.N.Y. 2025)
actions.factual_pattern: Settlement approved December 2025 for use of Meta Pixel on scientificamerican.com pages displaying video content. Defendant transmitted Facebook ID, video titles, and URLs to Meta without consent. $900,000 class settlement plus $200k+ in attorneys' fees.
remediation_imposed: Pixel suppression on video pages where the URL identifies the video content
Preserved ability to use Pixel with VPPA-compliant consent or in non-identifying configurations
actions.citation: Lee v. Springer Nature America, Inc., 2025 WL 3523134 (S.D.N.Y.).
operational_lessons: Even smaller subscriber-based publications face six-figure VPPA exposure.
Settlement framework — pixel suppression on identifying-URL pages, with consent or non-identifying-config carve-out — has become standardized.
Filed in S.D.N.Y. but settled before Solomon decision crystallized; subsequent S.D.N.Y. cases will face the Solomon defense at motion to dismiss.

actions.case_name: Rogers v. BNSF Railway Co. (BIPA)
actions.full_caption: Rogers v. BNSF Railway Co., No. 1:19-cv-03083 (N.D. Ill.)
actions.factual_pattern: Truck drivers using BNSF facilities were required to provide fingerprints to access gates. BIPA class action established that vendor-deployed biometric collection still implicates the principal's BIPA liability. Trial returned $228 million verdict for the class; subsequent reduction to $75 million on remand. First BIPA trial verdict.
remediation_imposed: Class fund disbursement
actions.citation: Rogers v. BNSF Railway Co., 19-cv-03083 (N.D. Ill.); 7th Cir. affirmance and remand 2024.
operational_lessons: BIPA's per-violation statutory damages structure produces verdicts that dwarf most state privacy law settlements. Even after reduction, $75M for a class of approximately 45,000 drivers.
Vendor-deployed biometric collection (e.g., gate-access systems, timekeeping kiosks) exposes the principal to BIPA liability — vendor contracting alone does not transfer BIPA exposure.
Industries with workplace biometric deployments (logistics, healthcare, manufacturing, food service) face concentrated BIPA exposure for IL-based workers.

actions.case_name: BIPA Amendment (SB 2979) — Per-violation rule revision
actions.full_caption: Illinois SB 2979 (Public Act 103-0769)
regulator: Illinois General Assembly (legislative response to Cothron)
actions.factual_pattern: After Cothron v. White Castle (Ill. 2023) held that each scan or transmission constitutes a separate BIPA violation — exposing employers to multi-billion-dollar judgments — the Illinois legislature enacted SB 2979 in August 2024. The amendment limits recovery to a single violation per private entity per individual for collection or disclosure of biometric data via the same method. Amendment applies prospectively. Pre-amendment claims may still proceed under Cothron's per-scan rule. UPDATE (April 1, 2026): The U.S. Court of Appeals for the 7th Circuit, in a consolidated appeal of Clay v. Union Pacific R.R. Co., Willis v. ___, and Gregg v. Central Transport LLC, held that SB 2979 applies RETROACTIVELY to BIPA cases pending when the amendment was enacted. The 7th Circuit reasoned that the amendment is procedural/remedial under § 4 of the Illinois Statute on Statutes (which establishes a presumption of retroactivity for procedural amendments absent express legislative intent to the contrary). This ruling substantially reduces pre-amendment damages exposure: pre-Cothron 'per-scan' damages calculations no longer control even for cases pending at amendment, dramatically narrowing class-wide damages models.
remediation_imposed: Statutory amendment limits future damages exposure
actions.citation: Public Act 103-0769; Cothron v. White Castle Sys., 2023 IL 128004.
operational_lessons: The 2024 BIPA amendment substantially reduces forward-going exposure but does not eliminate it.
Pre-August 2024 conduct remains subject to Cothron's per-scan rule, meaning legacy fact patterns retain extreme damages exposure.

BIPA still requires written informed consent and a published retention schedule before any biometric collection. Compliance is straightforward operationally; non-compliance is now amplified by the legislative narrowing of damages — a sign that BIPA litigation may shift toward statutory-damages plaintiff strategies that have to factor a smaller per-class headline.

*Retroactivity update (April 2026)*: 7th Circuit held SB 2979 applies retroactively. Pre-amendment 'per-scan' damages models in pending cases are now superseded — single-violation rule applies regardless of when the underlying conduct occurred. This is a material change for compliance posture: pre-2024 BIPA exposure is now substantially lower than initially anticipated.

actions.case_name: In re Marriott International, Inc. and Starwood Hotels & Resorts Worldwide LLC
actions.full_caption: In the Matter of Marriott International, Inc. and Starwood Hotels & Resorts Worldwide, LLC, FTC File No. 1923215
actions.respondent: Marriott International, Inc.; Starwood Hotels & Resorts Worldwide, LLC
statutes: Various state breach-notification and consumer-protection statutes
actions.factual_pattern: FTC and parallel multistate AG settlement following the 2014-2018 breach affecting approximately 344 million Starwood guest records. Marriott acquired Starwood in 2016 with the breach undetected; breach disclosed November 2018. FTC findings: inadequate security controls (no MFA, weak passwords, network segmentation failures, log management gaps), inadequate due diligence on Starwood's security posture during the acquisition.
remediation_imposed: Comprehensive information-security program with biennial third-party assessments for 20 years
MFA for all administrative accounts
Network segmentation
Data-mapping and minimization program
Consumer right to request deletion of personal information (notable — extends consumer rights beyond underlying state-law applicability)
actions.citation: FTC Decision and Order Oct 9, 2024; Multistate AG Settlement Oct 9, 2024.
operational_lessons: M&A due diligence on cybersecurity is operational expectation, not optional. Acquiring an entity with undetected breach exposure transfers liability.
FTC's deletion-right remedy in the Marriott consent order is significant: it imposes a CCPA-style consumer right on a respondent regardless of whether comprehensive privacy law applies.
The 49-AG multistate model concentrates leverage; Marriott's combined federal + state exposure is the operative figure, not the FTC penalty alone.
Hospitality and travel sectors are recurring targets — large legacy databases of identification and payment data make them high-priority enforcement focus.

actions.case_name: In re 23andMe, Inc. Customer Data Security Breach Litigation (MDL 3098) + multistate bankruptcy intervention
actions.full_caption: In re 23andMe, Inc. Cust. Data Sec. Breach Litig., MDL No. 3098 (N.D. Cal., Hon. Edward M. Chen) + Multistate AG Objection in In re Chrome Holding Co. (E.D. Mo. Bankruptcy Court, Case No. 25-40976-357)
regulator: Multistate AG coalition (28+ states, led by Oregon AG)
actions.respondent: 23andMe Holding Co. and 23andMe, Inc. (now Chrome Holding Co.)
statutes: Various state genetic privacy statutes (CA Genetic Information Privacy Act, NY Genetic Information Nondiscrimination Act, IL Genetic Information Privacy Act, AK Genetic Privacy Act)
California Confidentiality of Medical Information Act (CMIA) (Cal. Civ. Code § 56 et seq.)
actions.factual_pattern: October 6, 2023 credential-stuffing attack exposed personal information of approximately 6.4 million U.S. 23andMe customers, including raw genetic data, ancestry reports, health predisposition reports, family-tree and DNA-relatives data, and ethnic-heritage information. Threat actors specifically targeted users of Chinese and Ashkenazi Jewish ancestry; data was offered for sale on dark-web forums. Two parallel tracks resulted: (1) PRIVATE CLASS ACTION MDL: 40+ class actions consolidated April 2024 in N.D. Cal. (Judge Chen). Initial $30M settlement preliminarily approved December 2024. After 23andMe's March 23, 2025 Chapter 11 filing in E.D. Mo. (Case No. 25-40976-357), the company was sold July 2025 for $305M to a nonprofit led by former CEO Anne Wojcicki (renamed Chrome Holding Co.). Sale enabled an expanded $50M revised settlement preliminarily approved by bankruptcy court October 2, 2025; final approval hearing January 20, 2026. Settlement includes cash reimbursements + 5-year Privacy & Medical Shield + Genetic Monitoring program through CyEx. (2) MULTISTATE AG INTERVENTION: On June 9, 2025, a coalition of 28+ states (led by Oregon AG Dan Rayfield) filed an objection in the bankruptcy court opposing the proposed sale of customer genetic data to a successor without express consumer consent — arguing that genetic data is too sensitive to be sold as ordinary property. AG coalition position: customers must have right to control, with informed consent, the use of their genetic data; if buyers cannot or will not obtain such consent, the data must not be sold.
remediation_imposed: $50M class action settlement fund (revised; preliminarily approved October 2025)
Cash reimbursements for documented out-of-pocket expenses
5-year Privacy & Medical Shield + Genetic Monitoring (CyEx)
Mandatory MFA for all consumer accounts
Information-security program enhancements binding on successor entity (Chrome Holding Co.)
Express consumer consent required for any future transfer of genetic data (per multistate AG objection terms; pending bankruptcy court resolution)
Separate $3.25M (CAD $4.49M) settlement for 300,000 Canadian citizens
actions.citation: In re 23andMe, Inc. Cust. Data Sec. Breach Litig., MDL No. 3098 (N.D. Cal.); Oregon AG Press Release June 9, 2025 (multistate bankruptcy objection); In re Chrome Holding Co., Case No. 25-40976-357 (Bankr. E.D. Mo.).
operational_lessons: *Class action vs. AG enforcement distinction*: The $50M settlement is a class-action MDL settlement, not a multistate AG settlement. The multistate AG dimension is the bankruptcy intervention objecting to genetic-data transfer without consumer consent — a structural protection for affected consumers' future privacy interests, not a monetary recovery.

Bankruptcy does not extinguish state AG investigations or consumer-protection oversight — AGs may intervene to ensure consumer-protection remedies bind successor entities and to block asset sales that would prejudice consumer interests.

Genetic and biometric data warrant heightened security controls beyond ordinary PI. Credential-stuffing attacks against accounts holding sensitive data are now a consistent pattern triggering coordinated multistate response.

Direct-to-consumer genetic testing operators face a layered compliance environment: state-specific genetic privacy laws (CA GIPA, NY GINA, IL GIPA, AK Genetic Privacy Act) overlay comprehensive privacy laws, and ordinary commercial bankruptcy rules do not displace consumer-protection oversight.
MFA is now a baseline expectation for accounts containing sensitive PI; absence is increasingly characterized as 'unreasonable' under state breach-notification standards.
When a regulated entity files for Chapter 11, expect AG coalitions to enter the bankruptcy proceeding to secure consumer protections in any sale order — bankruptcy is not a privacy-law safe harbor.

actions.case_name: NY AG / DFS v. GEICO and Travelers (Data Security)
actions.full_caption: NY OAG and Department of Financial Services Settlements with GEICO and Travelers
regulator: New York Department of Financial Services
actions.factual_pattern: Joint OAG and DFS action over credential-stuffing attacks against insurance quote portals that exposed driver license numbers and other PI of thousands of New Yorkers. GEICO and Travelers had inadequate authentication and monitoring controls.
remediation_imposed: Comprehensive cybersecurity program enhancements
Mandatory bot-detection and credential-stuffing protections on consumer-facing portals
Annual reporting to OAG and DFS
actions.citation: NY OAG / DFS Press Release November 2024.
operational_lessons: NY's DFS Cybersecurity Regulation (23 NYCRR Part 500) creates parallel cybersecurity exposure for any insurer, bank, or other DFS-licensed entity, separate from CCPA-style comprehensive law applicability.
Credential-stuffing attacks are now a recognized pattern that AGs treat as foreseeable — failure to implement bot detection on quote portals is treated as 'unreasonable security.'
Consumer-facing PI quote portals (insurance quote tools, mortgage prequalification tools) are a repeated attack surface.

actions.case_name: In re Root Insurance (NY OAG Assurance of Discontinuance)
actions.full_caption: Assurance of Discontinuance — Root Insurance Company (NY OAG, March 20, 2025)
regulator: NY Office of the Attorney General
actions.factual_pattern: March 20, 2025 settlement. Root Insurance — a direct-to-consumer auto insurer that does not write policies in NY — exposed approximately 45,000 New York residents' driver's license numbers and personal information through a vulnerability in its online quoting application. The system 'pre-filled' driver's license numbers in a downloadable PDF, in plain text, after users entered limited public-record data. Threat actors used automated bots to harvest this information from January 2021 onward. Stolen NY DLNs were used to file fraudulent unemployment claims at the height of the COVID-19 pandemic. NY OAG investigation found Root failed to maintain reasonable security controls under the SHIELD Act. Settlement: $975,000 in penalties + remedial measures.
remediation_imposed: Comprehensive information security program
Encryption of personal information at rest and in transit
Access controls and authentication enhancements
Logging and monitoring
Threat response procedures
Free credit monitoring for affected NY residents
actions.citation: Assurance of Discontinuance, Root Insurance Co. (NY OAG, Mar. 20, 2025); NY OAG Press Release Mar. 20, 2025.
operational_lessons: An out-of-state company that does not transact business in NY can still face NY OAG enforcement when its security failures expose NY residents — territorial reach follows the residency of affected consumers.

Pre-fill vulnerabilities in quote applications, particularly those exposing government IDs in downloadable artifacts, are a recurring failure pattern across the auto-insurance breach line (Root, GEICO, Travelers, Noblr, plus pending Allstate matter — combined NY OAG recoveries now exceed $20.79M from 10 auto insurers).
Clear-text exposure of driver's license numbers in PDFs generated server-side is a per-se SHIELD Act 'reasonable security' failure regardless of intent.
Industry-wide attack patterns produce industry-wide enforcement: NY OAG has methodically worked through the entire auto-insurance quoting-tool breach line.

actions.case_name: NY v. Allstate Insurance Co. (data security litigation, pending)
actions.full_caption: People of the State of New York v. Allstate Insurance Co. (filed March 2025, pending)
regulator: NY Office of the Attorney General
actions.factual_pattern: March 2025 lawsuit (litigation pending — NOT yet settled). NY AG sued Allstate alleging that the company exposed approximately 165,000 New York residents' personal information through online quoting tool security failures, as part of the same industry-wide credential-stuffing and pre-fill-vulnerability attack pattern that produced the GEICO/Travelers ($11.3M, Nov 2024), Noblr ($500k, Dec 2024), and Root ($975k, Mar 2025) settlements. As of corpus date, the Allstate matter remains in active litigation.
remediation_imposed: Pending — litigation has not resolved
actions.citation: NY OAG complaint filed March 2025; NY OAG Press Releases referencing pending Allstate matter (e.g., Mar. 20, 2025; Oct. 20, 2025).
operational_lessons: Pending NY OAG litigation against a major insurer for the same fact pattern resolved in $20M+ of prior auto-insurance settlements signals that NY's enforcement posture remains aggressive and consistent.

Position of Allstate as the largest unresolved matter in this enforcement line means current settlement values ($500k–$11.3M for the others) likely under-state Allstate's eventual exposure given the 165,000-affected-NYer count.
Active litigation status is a citable distinction — when forecasting exposure, analysts should not collapse pending matters with settled ones.

actions.case_name: In re Rite Aid Corporation
actions.full_caption: In the Matter of Rite Aid Corporation, FTC File No. 2023-190
actions.factual_pattern: Rite Aid deployed AI-driven facial recognition technology in over 200 stores from 2012-2020 to identify suspected shoplifters, with disproportionate flagging of Black, Latino, Asian, and women shoppers based on inadequate model training and unreliable matching. Customers were searched, accused of crimes, and asked to leave stores based on facial-recognition matches. The FTC also found Rite Aid did not provide notice required under the FCRA when it used facial-recognition data to take adverse actions.
remediation_imposed: Five-year ban on use of facial-recognition technology in any retail store
Requirement to delete biometric data and any models trained on it
Comprehensive privacy program for any future biometric use
actions.citation: FTC Decision and Order December 2023.
operational_lessons: Facial recognition deployments in retail face per-se FTC scrutiny when the technology produces discriminatory outcomes.
Algorithmic-deletion remedies — destruction of models trained on tainted data — are now a recurring feature of FTC orders. Cf. Avast (2024).
FCRA's adverse-action notice requirements apply to facial-recognition-driven business decisions if the recognition output is treated as a 'consumer report' input.

actions.case_name: Florida v. TikTok / Snap (FDBR + Online Protections for Minors)
actions.full_caption: State of Florida v. TikTok Inc.; State of Florida v. Snap Inc. (Fla. Cir. Ct.)
actions.respondent: TikTok Inc.; Snap Inc.; ByteDance Ltd.
statutes: Fla. Deceptive and Unfair Trade Practices Act
actions.factual_pattern: Florida AG investigations and lawsuits launched 2024-2025 against TikTok, Snap, and other social platforms over alleged failures to protect minor users — engagement-maximizing algorithms targeting minors, default-on personalization, ad-targeting to known minors, and account-restriction tooling deficiencies. AG combines FDBR + Online Protections for Minors statute + DUTPA for compounded exposure.
remediation_imposed: Litigation pending
actions.citation: Fla. AG Press Releases 2024-2025.
operational_lessons: FDBR's $50,000 base penalty trebled to $150,000 for known violations involving minor data is the highest per-violation exposure of any state privacy law.
Florida combines comprehensive privacy (FDBR), sectoral minor-protection statutes, and DUTPA for compounded exposure — single fact patterns reach multiple statutes.
Platforms below FDBR's $1B threshold may still be reachable via parallel minor-protection statutes that have lower applicability thresholds.

actions.case_name: CO CPA Rules — Universal Opt-Out and Profiling Amendments
actions.full_caption: Colorado AG Final Rules amending 4 CCR § 904-3 (2024-2025 amendments)
actions.factual_pattern: CO AG finalized amendments to the CPA Rules expanding the UOOM list (currently includes GPC; recognized additions under review), tightening profiling-rights requirements, and incorporating CO's amended minor-protection provisions. Cure period sunset January 1, 2025; CO AG enforcement now without statutory cure right.
remediation_imposed: Industry compliance with new rules
actions.citation: Colorado AG Final Rules; 4 CCR § 904-3 (current).
operational_lessons: CO Rules remain the most prescriptive state privacy regulatory text. Multi-state controllers continue to find that aligning to CO rules satisfies most other states' less-prescriptive requirements.
Cure period sunset January 1, 2025 — direct enforcement now available; any post-2025 violation faces immediate penalty exposure.
UOOM list maintenance: controllers must monitor the AG's published list of recognized signals (currently GPC; potential additions like Apple's intelligent privacy signals being studied).
Profiling rights under CPA Rules § 9 require human-in-the-loop review for legal/significant decisions — distinguishing CO from most other states' less-prescriptive profiling provisions.

actions.case_name: NY OAG Settlement — Wojeski & Co. (Accounting Firm Data Breach)
actions.full_caption: NY OAG Settlement with Wojeski & Company
statutes: NY Gen. Bus. Law § 899-aa (Information Security Breach and Notification Act)
actions.factual_pattern: Settlement with regional accounting firm following two cybersecurity incidents that exposed personal information of 4,700+ New Yorkers. The OAG investigation found Wojeski took over a year to notify victims of the data breach despite NY's prompt-notification requirement. Demonstrates that small/mid-market professional services firms face direct AG exposure for breach response failures.
remediation_imposed: Comprehensive information-security program
One year of credit monitoring for affected individuals
Future breach-notification compliance commitments
actions.citation: NY OAG Press Release October 2025.
operational_lessons: Mid-market professional services firms are within scope. The $60,000 settlement is modest but the AG investigation cost likely exceeded that — reputational damage and operational disruption are the larger cost.
Late breach notification (over a year) is a separate violation distinct from the underlying breach. Statutory notification windows are strict-liability.
Accounting firms with client tax data, financial data, and SSNs face concentrated exposure regardless of CCPA-style applicability.

actions.case_name: In re InMarket Media, LLC
actions.full_caption: In the Matter of InMarket Media, LLC, FTC File No. 2123206
actions.factual_pattern: FTC settlement announced January 2024 prohibiting InMarket from selling precise consumer location data. Company collected location data via SDK embedded in third-party apps; consumers had no meaningful notice or consent for the data's use in advertising. Companion to the X-Mode/Outlogic, Mobilewalla, and Gravy Analytics matters.
remediation_imposed: Permanent ban on sale of precise location data
Deletion of historical location data and any audience segments derived from it
actions.citation: FTC Press Release January 18, 2024.
operational_lessons: SDK-distributed location data is treated identically to direct collection — the SDK provider is the controller of the data, not the host app.
InMarket's consent representations were rejected because the SDK relied on host-app TOS rather than InMarket's own affirmative-consent process. SDK providers must implement their own consent layer.
The Kochava-X-Mode-InMarket-Mobilewalla-Gravy line of cases now provides a coherent doctrinal framework that state AGs are importing into their own enforcement programs.
