actions.case_name: Maxwell v. Amazon.com, Inc. (first MHMDA private class action)
actions.full_caption: Maxwell v. Amazon.com, Inc. and Amazon Advertising LLC, No. 2:25-cv-00261 (W.D. Wash. filed Feb. 10, 2025)
actions.respondent: Amazon.com, Inc. and Amazon Advertising, LLC
statutes: Washington My Health My Data Act (Wash. Rev. Code §§ 19.373.005 et seq.)
Washington Consumer Protection Act (Wash. Rev. Code § 19.86)
Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (18 U.S.C. § 1030)
actions.factual_pattern: First federal lawsuit under Washington's My Health My Data Act (MHMDA), filed February 10, 2025 in the U.S. District Court for the Western District of Washington. NOTE: This is a PRIVATE CLASS ACTION (not a state AG enforcement action). Plaintiff Cassaundra Maxwell alleges Amazon.com and Amazon Advertising harvested precise geolocation data from tens of millions of Americans through Amazon's SDK embedded in 10,000+ third-party Android and iOS apps (including Weather Channel, OfferUp, GasBuddy). Theory: location data revealing visits to healthcare facilities is 'consumer health data' under MHMDA § 19.373.010, and Amazon failed to obtain consumer consent or make required disclosures. MHMDA violations are predicate UDAP violations under WA CPA § 19.86, with treble damages up to $25,000 per consumer plus attorney's fees and injunctive relief. Litigation pending — no monetary outcome yet.
remediation_imposed: Litigation pending — no resolution yet
actions.citation: Maxwell v. Amazon.com, Inc., No. 2:25-cv-00261 (W.D. Wash. Feb. 10, 2025).
operational_lessons: MHMDA's broad private right of action — coupled with WA CPA's treble-damages mechanism — creates substantial litigation exposure for any entity collecting precise geolocation data from consumers in WA, regardless of size or industry.

First-of-its-kind nature: this is the *first* federal MHMDA case, so judicial interpretation of the 'consumer health data' definition (whether mere geolocation is sufficient absent visit-to-sensitive-location proof) remains unresolved — survival of motion to dismiss will be a key inflection point.

Distinction from state AG enforcement: private actions under MHMDA can be pursued by individual consumers without the resource and prioritization constraints of state AG offices, meaning enforcement risk is independent of regulatory bandwidth.

SDK providers face direct liability for downstream collection patterns even when consumers interact only with the host app — the 'controller-processor' line under MHMDA tracks the actual data-flow, not contractual arrangements.

actions.case_name: In re Albany ENT & Allergy Services, P.C. (NY OAG)
actions.full_caption: Assurance of Discontinuance — Albany ENT & Allergy Services, P.C. (NY OAG, October 2024)
regulator: NY Office of the Attorney General
statutes: HIPAA Security Rule (45 C.F.R. § 164.308 et seq.) (referenced in factual record)
actions.factual_pattern: October 2024 settlement. Albany ENT & Allergy Services, P.C. (AENT), a Capital Region NY healthcare provider, suffered two cyberattacks in 2023 that compromised protected health information (PHI) of 213,935 New York residents. Records included names, addresses, dates of birth, driver's license numbers, social security numbers, diagnoses, conditions, lab results, medications, and treatment information. AENT outsourced its information security program to two third-party vendors and did not employ anyone with information security expertise internally. NY OAG investigation found AENT failed to monitor its third-party vendors, who in turn did not timely install critical security updates, did not encrypt PHI before or after the attacks, did not implement MFA for remote access, and did not maintain reasonable logging and monitoring. AENT's data storage devices continued to host unprotected PHI for months after the ransomware incidents. Settlement structure: $1,000,000 cash penalty (with $500,000 suspended), plus a mandatory $2.25 million investment in information security program over 5 years, plus 1 year of free credit monitoring for affected consumers. Total cash penalty: $500,000 (with another $500k suspended). Total mandated security spend: $2.25M.
remediation_imposed: $500,000 cash penalty (with $500,000 additional suspended pending compliance)
$2,250,000 mandatory investment in information security program over 5 years
Hire dedicated information security personnel (cease sole reliance on third-party vendors for security function)
Implement encryption of PHI at rest and in transit
Implement MFA for all remote access
Implement comprehensive logging and monitoring
Implement vendor management program with security oversight
1 year of free credit monitoring for affected consumers
actions.citation: Assurance of Discontinuance, Albany ENT & Allergy Services, P.C. (NY OAG, Oct. 2024); NY OAG Press Release Oct. 2024.
operational_lessons: *Settlement structure clarification*: NY OAG settlements often distinguish cash penalties from mandated security investment. The widely-cited '$2.25M Capital Region' figure is the *security investment*, not the cash penalty; cash penalty was $500k (with another $500k suspended).

Outsourcing security to third-party vendors does not transfer accountability — covered entities remain responsible for vendor performance under both SHIELD Act 'reasonable security' and HIPAA Security Rule § 164.308(b)(1) (business associate oversight).

Continuing to host unprotected PHI months after a known ransomware incident (no remediation, no encryption, no access-control hardening) is a categorical 'reasonable security' failure that supports per-violation penalty analysis.
MFA, encryption, and logging are now baseline expectations for healthcare data — absence in any of these three areas is a near-automatic finding under SHIELD Act § 899-bb.

actions.case_name: Doe v. Kaiser Foundation Health Plan, Inc. (CIPA / ECPA pixel class action)
actions.full_caption: In re Kaiser Permanente Tracking Technology Litig.; consolidated class action involving Kaiser Permanente patient portal tracking
actions.factual_pattern: December 2025 settlement for $46-47.5 million covering approximately 13.4 million Kaiser members who accessed authenticated patient portals between 2017-2024. Plaintiffs alleged Kaiser embedded session-replay code from Quantum Metric and advertising pixels from Google, Bing, and Adobe on authenticated patient-portal pages, transmitting keystrokes, form entries, appointment requests, and messages to providers in real time. Kaiser had separately notified 13.4 million members in April 2024 that data may have been transmitted to third-party vendors. Settlement does not include admission of liability.
remediation_imposed: Settlement fund distribution to class
Pixel removal from authenticated patient-portal pages
Class certification did not need to be litigated to conclusion — settlement reached
actions.citation: In re Kaiser Permanente Tracking Tech. Litig.; settlement approved Dec 2025.
operational_lessons: Among the largest CIPA/ECPA class settlements involving healthcare pixel deployments. Healthcare-portal authenticated-page tracking is the highest-exposure CIPA fact pattern.
Kaiser settlement is a benchmark for valuation of similar matters: $46M / 13.4M class members ≈ $3.43/member as a settlement floor.

Session-replay tools (Quantum Metric, Hotjar, FullStory, LogRocket) deployed on healthcare-portal authenticated pages remain the highest-value plaintiff target despite recent narrowing rulings (Torres v. Prudential).

Authenticated patient portals are the boundary where HIPAA, CIPA, ECPA, and state-comprehensive privacy theories converge. Removing third-party pixels from authenticated environments is the single highest-leverage compliance action for healthcare entities.
Settlement timing illustrates that even with favorable defense rulings (Solomon v. Flipps, Torres v. Prudential), defendants in healthcare-pixel matters often settle rather than risk class trial.

actions.case_name: Frasco v. Flo Health, Inc. — Jury Verdict (CIPA SDK)
actions.full_caption: Frasco v. Flo Health, Inc., No. 3:21-cv-00757 (N.D. Cal., filed Jan. 29, 2021; verdict Aug. 1, 2025)
actions.respondent: Meta Platforms, Inc.; Flo Health, Inc.
actions.respondent_industry: Femtech / period and fertility tracking; social media
actions.factual_pattern: Jury verdict against Meta in 2025 finding that Meta's SDK captured user communications with the Flo Health period-tracking app in real time in violation of CIPA. Court denied Meta's renewed motion for judgment as a matter of law, holding: (1) capturing one party's communications is sufficient under CIPA; (2) a physical device is not necessary — a user's phone qualifies; (3) Meta actively encouraged Flo to integrate its SDK; (4) Meta's privacy policy disclosures were inadequate; (5) Flo's policy expressly assured users that data would not be shared with Meta. Verdict significant because it confirms CIPA reaches mobile-SDK deployments, not only website pixels, and rejects the 'no physical device' defense.
remediation_imposed: Damages to be determined; appeal expected
actions.citation: Frasco v. Flo Health, Inc., No. 3:21-cv-00757 (N.D. Cal. Aug. 1, 2025) (jury verdict).
operational_lessons: CIPA reaches mobile-SDK deployments, not only website pixels. Mobile app developers integrating Meta SDK, Google Analytics SDK, or similar must evaluate the same way as web pixel deployments.

Express privacy-policy assurances ('we will not share with Meta') that are contradicted by actual SDK behavior create both deception and CIPA exposure. Verify policy language matches actual deployment.
Affirmative SDK provider conduct (encouraging integration, providing documentation) is treated as evidence of intent to capture — provider liability extends beyond mere availability of the SDK.
Femtech and reproductive-health apps face particularly high CIPA / state sensitive-data exposure given the intimate nature of the data.

actions.case_name: Torres v. Prudential Financial, Inc. (Defense win on session replay)
actions.full_caption: Torres v. Prudential Fin., Inc., No. 22-CV-7465-CRB (N.D. Cal. Apr. 17, 2025)
regulator: Private class action — district court ruling
actions.factual_pattern: Summary judgment for defendants. Plaintiff filled out an online life-insurance quote form using ActiveProspect's TrustedForm session-replay software. Court held that CIPA § 631 requires that a defendant 'read or attempt to read' communication contents while in transit; session-replay data 'cannot be meaningfully read or understood until later, when a session replay is reassembled,' so the in-transit element is not satisfied. Court also previously denied class certification challenges in 2024 by holding CIPA violations confer Article III standing inherently (post TransUnion v. Ramirez analysis based on Calhoun v. Google).
remediation_imposed: Defense judgment
actions.citation: Torres v. Prudential Fin., Inc., 2025 WL 1135088 (N.D. Cal. Apr. 17, 2025).
operational_lessons: Significant defense ruling: session-replay tools that reassemble user interactions only after transmission do not satisfy CIPA § 631's 'in transit' requirement.

The ruling does not eliminate CIPA exposure for session replay — it depends on the technical specifics of the deployment. Tools that process or transmit human-readable data in real time remain at risk.
Compare to Heerde v. Learfield (C.D. Cal. 2024) (claim survived where search terms were transmitted in real time to third parties).
Plaintiffs continue to bring CIPA claims even where Torres-style defenses are available — the cost of defending past summary judgment motivates many controllers to settle anyway.

actions.case_name: Thomas v. Papa John's (9th Cir. defense affirmance)
actions.full_caption: Thomas v. Papa John's, 2025 WL 1704437 (9th Cir. June 18, 2025)
actions.factual_pattern: Ninth Circuit affirmed dismissal of CIPA class action. Plaintiff alleged Papa John's directly violated § 631(a) by 'eavesdropping' on user interactions via session-replay code. Panel held that 'a party to a conversation cannot be liable for eavesdropping on its own conversation' — a website operator that captures interactions on its own site is not eavesdropping under CIPA. The 'aiding and abetting' theory (operator helps a third party intercept) remains viable but the direct-party theory is foreclosed.
remediation_imposed: Defense judgment
actions.citation: Thomas v. Papa John's, 2025 WL 1704437 (9th Cir. June 18, 2025).
operational_lessons: Ninth Circuit forecloses the 'direct party' CIPA theory: a website operator capturing interactions on its own site is a party to those communications and cannot eavesdrop on itself.

Plaintiffs must rely on aiding-and-abetting theory: operator aids a third-party tool that itself intercepts. Cases now turn on whether the tool provider is acting as an extension of the operator (no liability) or as an independent third party (liability).
Forum matters substantially: 9th Circuit is more defense-favorable post-Thomas; 6th Circuit (Salazar v. Paramount) and 1st Circuit may be more plaintiff-favorable.

Combined with Solomon v. Flipps (2d Cir. VPPA), Thomas represents an emerging consensus narrowing class-action wiretap theories — but the underlying compliance picture has not changed for entities deploying third-party trackers.

actions.case_name: CIPA § 638.51 Pen Register Rulings (Palacios; Aviles; Khamooshi; Popa)
actions.full_caption: Various — Palacios v. Fandom, Aviles v. LiveRamp, Khamooshi v. Politico, Popa v. Microsoft
regulator: Private class action — district court rulings
actions.factual_pattern: Plaintiffs in 2024-2025 expanded CIPA litigation by adding § 638.51 'pen register / trap and trace' claims to existing § 631 wiretap claims. Multiple courts pushed back: (1) Palacios v. Fandom (Cal. Super. Ct. Sept. 2024) — IP addresses are not 'outgoing communications' as required for the pen register statute; (2) Aviles v. LiveRamp (Cal. Super. Ct. Jan. 2025) — tracking beacon collected only IP addresses and device information, did not qualify as a pen register; (3) Khamooshi v. Politico LLC (N.D. Cal. Oct. 2025) — no Article III standing for collection of generic device/browser metadata that is not 'embarrassing, invasive, or otherwise private.' Ninth Circuit's Popa v. Microsoft applied a narrowed standing standard.
remediation_imposed: Various — pleadings dismissed in defense favor
actions.citation: Palacios v. Fandom, No. 24STCV11264 (Cal. Super. Ct. 2024); Aviles v. LiveRamp, No. 23STCV28190 (Cal. Super. Ct. 2025); Khamooshi v. Politico LLC, 2025 WL 2822879 (N.D. Cal. 2025).
operational_lessons: CIPA § 638.51 'pen register' claims targeting tracking pixels and beacons have struggled in court. IP addresses, device IDs, and browser metadata generally don't satisfy the pen register definition.
Standing is increasingly contested — collection of routine device/browser metadata is increasingly held insufficient to support Article III injury under TransUnion v. Ramirez and Popa v. Microsoft.
Plaintiffs continue to file these claims because the cost of defense and class-certification risk creates settlement leverage even where the underlying theory is weak.
California SB 690 (Caballero) was intended to exclude routine commercial tracking from CIPA's scope but failed to advance in 2025 — now a 'two-year bill' unlikely to take effect before 2027.

actions.case_name: CIPA Chatbot / Customer Service Recording Class Actions (Various)
actions.full_caption: Various — Javier v. Assurance IQ; chatbot-CIPA litigation line
actions.respondent: Multiple (Assurance IQ, various chatbot operators and vendors)
actions.respondent_industry: Insurance, retail, hospitality, healthcare — any operator of a customer-facing chatbot
actions.factual_pattern: Mass-claim plaintiffs' bar has filed thousands of CIPA chatbot cases against operators that deploy live-chat or AI-chatbot widgets without all-parties consent. Foundational case: Javier v. Assurance IQ (9th Cir. 2022) (denying motion to dismiss in chatbot case based on pre-collection consent failure). Volume is sufficient that arbitration demand letters have been characterized as a mass-claim revenue stream. Generative-AI chatbots are now subject to similar legal theories.
remediation_imposed: Various — settled or dismissed individually
actions.citation: Javier v. Assurance IQ, LLC, 39 F.4th 519 (9th Cir. 2022); voluminous district court filings 2023-2025.
operational_lessons: Any customer-facing chatbot — live chat or generative AI — that captures California user inputs is potential CIPA exposure unless all-parties consent is obtained.
Pre-chat consent flows (banner, click-through, in-chat consent message before any user input) are the baseline compliance posture.

The mass-claim plaintiffs' bar uses CIPA's $5,000-per-violation statutory damages plus arbitration demand letters as a settlement-leverage strategy — even cases with weak merit produce settlement pressure due to defense costs.

Generative-AI chatbots inherit all the prior chatbot-CIPA exposure plus emerging concerns about training-data uses; controllers should evaluate both the conversation-recording and downstream-use angles.

actions.case_name: In re Rytr LLC (settled 2024; vacated December 2025)
actions.full_caption: In the Matter of Rytr LLC, FTC File No. 232-3052
actions.factual_pattern: September 2024 Operation AI Comply enforcement sweep. FTC alleged Rytr's AI-powered writing assistant included a 'Testimonial & Review' generation tool that produced detailed, materially-false consumer reviews from minimal prompts; some Rytr customers used it to generate thousands of false reviews. Original final consent order (Dec 2024) prohibited Rytr from offering any service for generating consumer reviews. CRITICAL UPDATE: On December 22, 2025, the new FTC majority issued an order to reopen and set aside the 2024 consent order, holding that the original complaint failed to plead consumer injury under the unfairness standard. Rytr matter is no longer good authority for FTC AI enforcement; the reversal signals a narrowed FTC AI-enforcement posture going forward.
remediation_imposed: Original order vacated December 2025
actions.citation: FTC Press Release Sept 25, 2024 (Operation AI Comply); FTC Order Reopening and Setting Aside Final Consent Order Dec 22, 2025.
operational_lessons: *Status warning*: The Rytr settlement was vacated December 2025. Do not cite as current authority for the proposition that 'AI generation of consumer reviews is per-se unfair.' The current FTC majority requires evidence of actual consumer injury, not theoretical potential for misuse.

Reversal is significant strategic signal: under Chairman Ferguson, the FTC is reviewing AI enforcement to prioritize 'deception' theories with concrete consumer harm over 'unfairness' theories targeting AI products' potential for misuse.

DoNotPay (deception-based) remains good authority — the distinction from Rytr is that DoNotPay actually misrepresented its AI lawyer's capability, while Rytr's product worked as advertised but could be misused.
AI vendors should still focus on substantiation of capability claims; deception-theory enforcement remains active even as unfairness-theory enforcement narrows.

actions.case_name: In re DoNotPay, Inc.
actions.full_caption: In the Matter of DoNotPay, Inc., FTC File No. 232-3009
actions.factual_pattern: September 2024 Operation AI Comply enforcement. DoNotPay marketed its subscription service as 'the world's first robot lawyer' and 'AI lawyer,' claiming to prepare 'ironclad' legal documents and check small-business websites for hundreds of federal and state law violations. FTC alleged DoNotPay did not test whether AI output was equivalent to human lawyer work and did not employ any attorneys. Settlement: $193,000 plus subscriber notice and prohibition on substituted-for-professional-service claims without substantiation. Notable: Commissioner Ferguson supported this matter (deception theory) even as he opposed Rytr (unfairness theory) — likely current FTC posture template.
remediation_imposed: Notice to 2021-2023 subscribers warning about limitations of law-related features
Prohibition on claims of substitution for professional services without substantiation
Compliance reporting
actions.citation: FTC Press Release Sept 25, 2024; Final Consent Order.
operational_lessons: DoNotPay illustrates the surviving FTC AI-enforcement model: deception theories grounded in unsubstantiated capability claims, not unfairness theories about AI products' misuse potential.

AI vendors must substantiate marketing claims about replacement of human professionals (lawyers, doctors, financial advisors). Marketing copy that says 'AI lawyer' or 'replaces your accountant' must be supported by testing and credentialed personnel.
5-0 Commission vote at the time signals bipartisan agreement — DoNotPay-style deception cases will likely continue under the current administration even as unfairness-based cases recede.
Privacy-AI vendors should ensure capability claims are substantiated and test results are documented before marketing to regulated buyers.

actions.case_name: FTC and Los Angeles County DA v. NGL Labs, LLC
actions.full_caption: Federal Trade Commission, et al. v. NGL Labs, LLC, et al., No. 2:24-cv-05753 (C.D. Cal., complaint filed July 9, 2024; stipulated order July 14, 2024)
actions.respondent: NGL Labs, LLC; co-founders Raj Vir and Joao Figueiredo
actions.factual_pattern: Joint FTC + Los Angeles County District Attorney action against NGL ('NGL: ask me anything') — anonymous messaging app marketed to children and teens. FTC alleged: (1) NGL sent users automated fake messages appearing to come from contacts to drive engagement; (2) NGL Pro subscription falsely promised to reveal sender identities; (3) NGL collected children's data in violation of COPPA without verifiable parental consent. Settlement permanently bars NGL from marketing to users under 18. January 2026 claims process for defrauded consumers.
remediation_imposed: Permanent ban on offering NGL or similar service to users under 18 (industry-first remedy)
Consumer redress for deceived subscribers
Personal liability for co-founders Vir and Figueiredo
actions.citation: FTC Press Release July 9, 2024; Stipulated Order, FTC, et al. v. NGL Labs, LLC, No. 2:24-cv-05753 (C.D. Cal. July 14, 2024).
operational_lessons: First FTC remedy permanently barring an app from offering its service to minors. The remedy structure is exportable to other apps deemed unsuitable for minors.

Personal liability for AI/app co-founders is on the table — Vir and Figueiredo were named individually. Cf. Cerebral (Robertson). Tech founders should not assume corporate veil protects from FTC enforcement when they actively directed the conduct.
Joint federal-state action model: FTC + California AG coordinated enforcement, sharing remedies and investigation. Multi-jurisdictional exposure is the operating model for child-data cases.
Anonymous messaging and minor-targeted apps face the highest enforcement scrutiny — particularly when AI-generated 'fake messages' simulate human contact.

actions.case_name: In re Evolv Technology, Inc.
actions.full_caption: In the Matter of Evolv Technology, Inc., FTC File No. 232-3050
actions.factual_pattern: December 2024 settlement. Evolv marketed AI-powered weapon-detection scanners to K-12 schools and other venues with claims of high accuracy and ability to distinguish weapons from common items. FTC alleged Evolv's scanners failed to detect weapons in real-world deployments and triggered alarms on harmless items, contradicting marketing claims. Order bans unsubstantiated detectability claims and gives certain K-12 customers the right to cancel contracts.
remediation_imposed: Permanent ban on unsubstantiated claims about detection capabilities
Contract-cancellation rights for K-12 school customers
Compliance reporting
actions.citation: FTC Press Release Dec 2024.
operational_lessons: AI capability claims directed at school-safety, healthcare, or other safety-critical contexts face heightened FTC scrutiny.
Customer cancellation rights are an emerging remedial structure — even where products are not removed from market, customers may regain contract flexibility.
'AI-powered' as a marketing label does not alter the underlying substantiation requirement. If the claim is 'detects weapons,' the product must do so reliably.
Privacy-AI vendors must verify accuracy claims with documented testing — particularly for products marketed to regulated buyers.

actions.case_name: In re IntelliVision Technologies Corp.
actions.full_caption: In the Matter of IntelliVision Technologies Corp., FTC File No. 222-3192
actions.factual_pattern: December 3, 2024 order. IntelliVision marketed its facial-recognition software to OEMs and consumers, claiming it 'identifies faces of all ethnicities without racial or gender bias' based on training on 'millions of images from across the world with a high accuracy rate.' FTC alleged these claims were unsubstantiated and that IntelliVision's bias-testing methodology was insufficient. Order requires substantiation for performance and bias-mitigation claims.
remediation_imposed: Prohibition on unsubstantiated bias-mitigation claims
Required substantiation methodology for performance claims
20-year compliance reporting
actions.citation: FTC Press Release Dec 3, 2024.
operational_lessons: FTC's expectations on AI bias claims are increasingly specific: vague representations of 'no bias' or 'high accuracy across ethnicities' must be backed by documented testing methodology and disclosed limitations.
Facial-recognition vendors face particularly high scrutiny given the Rite Aid (2023) action's structural prohibition on retail facial-recognition deployments.
Algorithm-deletion remedies extend to AI training data and derived models — controllers cannot simply 'fix' bias going forward; tainted models may need to be destroyed.
AI bias claims should be treated as substantiated marketing representations requiring documented evidentiary support, not aspirational language.

actions.case_name: Texas v. Pieces Technologies (AI healthcare)
actions.full_caption: Texas v. Pieces Technologies, Inc., Assurance of Voluntary Compliance
actions.factual_pattern: September 18, 2024 settlement. First-of-its-kind state AG enforcement against a generative-AI healthcare company. Texas AG alleged Pieces Technologies made deceptive and misleading statements about the accuracy and safety of its products that were used by clinicians in patient-care settings. AG focused on representations of 'severe hallucination rate' as below industry threshold without substantiation. Settlement requires Pieces to disclose accuracy metrics and limitations to customers.
remediation_imposed: Required disclosure of testing methodology and accuracy metrics to customers
Required disclosure of limitations and known failure modes
Compliance reporting
actions.citation: Tex. AG Press Release Sept 18, 2024; Assurance of Voluntary Compliance.
operational_lessons: First state-AG enforcement of an AI healthcare product on misrepresentation grounds. Templates the model that other state AGs will follow.
AI hallucination rates and safety metrics are now a regulated marketing dimension — vendors must substantiate and disclose them.
Texas AG is willing to lead on AI enforcement under DTPA-style consumer protection theories, paralleling its TDPSA enforcement aggressiveness.

AI in healthcare clinical operations has overlapping exposure: state AG (consumer protection / deception), FDA (depending on product classification), HHS OCR (where PHI is processed), state medical boards. Vendors must coordinate compliance across all four.

actions.case_name: In re Block, Inc. (Cash App) — CFPB Consent Order
actions.full_caption: In the Matter of Block, Inc., CFPB Consent Order 2025-CFPB-0001
actions.factual_pattern: January 16, 2025 CFPB consent order. Block ordered to pay up to $120 million in consumer redress and a $55 million civil penalty for inadequate security and dispute-handling on Cash App. CFPB alleged: (1) weak security protocols leading to widespread fraud exposure; (2) misrepresentations in TOS that Cash App's banks (rather than Cash App itself) were responsible for fraud investigation under EFTA; (3) shoddy fraud investigations consistently resolved in the company's favor; (4) deliberate friction in customer support to discourage fraud claims. Parallel state regulator action: $80 million multistate settlement with 48 state regulators for BSA/AML violations (January 2025).
remediation_imposed: Up to $120M consumer redress through CFPB victims relief fund
$55M civil penalty
Required overhaul of fraud investigation procedures
24/7 customer support implementation
Mandatory bot-detection / authentication enhancements
Compliance reporting
actions.citation: CFPB Press Release Jan 16, 2025; Consent Order.
operational_lessons: Fintech privacy/security failures face direct CFPB exposure plus parallel state-financial-regulator action — combined exposure exceeds CCPA-style penalties for similar conduct.
TOS allocations of fraud-investigation responsibility to consumer banks are unenforceable under EFTA — fintechs are the responsible party.
Customer-support obstruction (deliberate friction in fraud reporting) is a separate consumer-protection violation distinct from the underlying security failure.
Combined CFPB + 48-state-regulator + private-class-action exposure produces a cumulative figure ($295M+) that dwarfs any single-regulator settlement.
Fintech vendors face overlapping CFPB, state-financial-regulator, FTC, and state-AG exposure for the same fact patterns.

actions.case_name: NY DFS PayPal Settlement — 23 NYCRR Part 500 Cybersecurity Regulation
actions.full_caption: In the Matter of PayPal, Inc., NYDFS Consent Order
regulator: New York Department of Financial Services
actions.factual_pattern: January 2025 NYDFS consent order. NYDFS alleged PayPal failed to implement adequate cybersecurity controls under Part 500 leading to a December 2022 data exposure incident affecting Social Security Numbers and other PI of approximately 35,000 customers. Specific violations: inadequate access controls; inadequate multi-factor authentication; insufficient training of personnel handling customer data. Settlement: $2 million civil penalty plus mandated cybersecurity program enhancements.
remediation_imposed: Comprehensive Part 500 cybersecurity program
Mandatory MFA for accounts containing sensitive data
Enhanced personnel training
Two-year compliance reporting
actions.citation: NYDFS Press Release January 2025.
operational_lessons: NYDFS's Part 500 Cybersecurity Regulation is enforced separately from CCPA-style comprehensive privacy laws and applies to all DFS-licensed entities (banks, insurers, mortgage brokers, money transmitters, virtual currency businesses).

Part 500's recent amendments (effective November 2023 with phased implementation) tighten MFA, access control, and CISO governance requirements. Coverage now includes 'Class A' larger entities with heightened standards.
Annual CISO attestations under Part 500 are now scrutinized — false attestations create independent liability for the certifying officer.
DFS-licensed entities face dual exposure: NYDFS Part 500 + state breach-notification statutes (NY GBL § 899-aa, others) + state AG consumer-protection theories.

actions.case_name: In re R.R. Donnelley & Sons Co. (SEC Cybersecurity Disclosure)
actions.full_caption: In the Matter of R.R. Donnelley & Sons Co., Securities Exchange Act Release No. 100365
actions.factual_pattern: June 2024 SEC settlement. SEC alleged RRD failed to maintain adequate disclosure controls and procedures around cybersecurity incidents, including the November 2021 ransomware attack that affected 70+ business units and exposed approximately 70,000 individuals' data. SEC focused on the lag between incident detection and corporate disclosure, and the inadequacy of internal escalation procedures. Settlement: $2.125 million civil penalty.
remediation_imposed: Disclosure controls overhaul
Incident-escalation procedures with materiality assessment timelines
Compliance reporting
actions.citation: SEC Press Release June 18, 2024.
operational_lessons: Public companies face SEC exposure for cybersecurity disclosure failures separate from FTC, state AG, or state-DFS exposure.

Item 1.05 Form 8-K (effective December 2023) requires disclosure of material cybersecurity incidents within four business days of materiality determination — but the SEC scrutinizes the *materiality determination process itself*, not just the disclosure timing.

Privacy/security incidents at public companies trigger overlapping enforcement: SEC (disclosure), FTC (consumer protection), state AGs (breach notification), and potentially private securities class actions.
Disclosure controls and procedures (DCP) under Exchange Act Rule 13a-15 are an SEC-specific compliance dimension that must integrate with security-incident response.

actions.case_name: FTC GLBA Safeguards Rule Enforcement — Pattern of Recent Actions
actions.full_caption: Various — FTC Safeguards Rule enforcement under 16 C.F.R. Part 314
actions.respondent_industry: Auto dealers, mortgage brokers, payday lenders, tax preparers, debt collectors
actions.factual_pattern: FTC Safeguards Rule expanded scope and content effective June 9, 2023. Required elements: written information security program; designated qualified individual responsible; risk assessments; access controls; encryption of customer information at rest and in transit; secure development practices; MFA; data disposal procedures; change management; monitoring and testing; staff training; service-provider oversight; incident response plan; CISO board reporting. FTC has pursued enforcement against non-bank financial institutions (auto dealers, mortgage companies, tax preparers) for non-compliance, particularly post-breach.
remediation_imposed: Various — Safeguards Rule compliance programs
actions.citation: FTC Safeguards Rule, 16 C.F.R. Part 314 (effective June 9, 2023).
operational_lessons: GLBA applies to a broader range of 'financial institutions' than commonly assumed — including auto dealers, mortgage brokers, tax preparers, debt collectors, money transmitters, and other non-bank entities.
The expanded Safeguards Rule (June 2023) created GDPR-style detailed security requirements that state AG enforcement under sensitive-data theories now imports.
FTC Safeguards Rule + state breach-notification + state-comprehensive-privacy law (where applicable) creates layered exposure for any non-bank financial entity.

Non-bank financial institutions are typically exempt from CCPA and other state comprehensive privacy laws (GLBA exemption) — but the Safeguards Rule itself remains enforceable, and HR/B2B/marketing data may not be GLBA-exempt at the data level.

actions.case_name: NYDFS Cybersecurity Pattern — GEICO, Travelers, PayPal, Healthplex (2023-2025)
actions.full_caption: Various NYDFS Part 500 enforcement actions
regulator: New York Department of Financial Services
actions.respondent: Multiple — GEICO; Travelers; PayPal; Healthplex; others
actions.factual_pattern: Pattern of NYDFS Part 500 enforcement against DFS-licensed entities for credential-stuffing and ransomware exposures. GEICO and Travelers ($11.3M, November 2024) for credential-stuffing exposures via insurance quote portals. PayPal ($2M, January 2025) for inadequate access controls. Healthplex ($400k, 2024) for ransomware response failures. Pattern: NYDFS targets quote-portal exposures, MFA gaps, and slow incident reporting under Part 500's 72-hour notification window.
remediation_imposed: Various — Part 500 program enhancements
Cybersecurity certification re-attestations
Two-year compliance reporting in most matters
actions.citation: NYDFS Press Releases 2023-2025.
operational_lessons: NYDFS Part 500 is the most-enforced state cybersecurity regulation. Pattern of enforcement against DFS-licensed entities is now several actions per year.

Insurance quote portals, mortgage application systems, and customer self-service portals are recurring credential-stuffing targets — bot detection and MFA on these surfaces is now a Part 500 expectation.
Part 500's 72-hour notice requirement is enforced separately from breach-notification timelines; entities must notify NYDFS even when a state breach-notification clock has not yet started.
CISO attestations (for 'Class A' entities) are scrutinized; entities with materially deficient programs cannot lawfully attest compliance and signing officer faces individual liability.

actions.case_name: American Hospital Association v. Becerra (HIPAA pixel guidance vacated)
actions.full_caption: Am. Hosp. Ass'n v. Becerra, No. 4:23-cv-1110 (N.D. Tex. June 20, 2024)
regulator: Federal court ruling against HHS OCR
actions.respondent: U.S. Department of Health & Human Services, Office for Civil Rights (OCR)
actions.factual_pattern: AHA, Texas Hospital Association, and two hospital systems sued HHS to vacate OCR's December 2022 / March 2024 'Online Tracking Technologies Bulletin.' The Bulletin asserted that HIPAA obligations are triggered when an online technology connects (1) an individual's IP address with (2) a visit to an unauthenticated public webpage addressing specific health conditions or healthcare providers (the 'Proscribed Combination'). N.D. Tex. court ruled June 20, 2024 that the Proscribed Combination exceeded OCR's HIPAA rulemaking authority, was procedurally invalid, and was vacated. OCR withdrew its appeal August 29, 2024.
remediation_imposed: OCR Bulletin Proscribed Combination vacated as to unauthenticated webpages
actions.citation: Am. Hosp. Ass'n v. Becerra, No. 4:23-cv-1110, 2024 WL 3075865 (N.D. Tex. June 20, 2024).
operational_lessons: AHA v. Becerra narrowed HIPAA's reach to authenticated patient-portal pages and clearly-PHI-disclosing unauthenticated pages. OCR cannot enforce HIPAA against pixel deployments on generic public webpages merely because they discuss health conditions.

The ruling does NOT eliminate state-law exposure for the same conduct: state sensitive-data definitions (CO, CT, VA, OR, MN, MD), CIPA wiretap theories, FTC § 5 deception, and MHMDA all continue to reach the conduct AHA v. Becerra removed from HIPAA's scope.

Healthcare entities should not interpret AHA v. Becerra as a pixel safe harbor. Authenticated patient portals remain unambiguously within HIPAA, and unauthenticated pages with clearly-PHI-disclosing URLs (e.g., '/oncology-treatment-options' followed by appointment-request behavior) likely remain within scope.
OCR's Bulletin was vacated; OCR has not yet issued formal rulemaking to fill the gap. Healthcare entities operating pixels on unauthenticated pages should evaluate the post-AHA position with counsel.
Cf. Doe v. Meta MDL (CIPA pixel theory against hospitals separately) — even where OCR cannot enforce HIPAA, plaintiffs continue private CIPA / state-law actions for the same conduct.

actions.case_name: In re Warby Parker (OCR HIPAA Cybersecurity)
actions.full_caption: OCR Civil Money Penalty against Warby Parker, Inc.
statutes: HIPAA Security Rule (45 C.F.R. Part 164, Subpart C)
actions.factual_pattern: February 20, 2025 OCR civil money penalty. Warby Parker operates as a HIPAA-covered entity for its vision-care services. Following a 2018 credential-stuffing attack affecting 197,000+ individuals' PHI, OCR imposed a $1.5 million civil money penalty for HIPAA Security Rule violations including: failure to conduct accurate and thorough risk analysis; failure to implement appropriate technical safeguards; failure to monitor information system activity; failure to implement procedures for password management.
remediation_imposed: $1.5M civil money penalty (after Warby Parker did not contest)
Mandatory compliance with HIPAA Security Rule risk analysis requirements
actions.citation: OCR Press Release Feb 20, 2025.
operational_lessons: Risk analysis failures are the #1 issue cited in OCR enforcement. Entities lacking documented, current risk analyses (including post-acquisition risk re-analysis) face substantial penalty exposure.

Credential-stuffing attacks are a recurring HIPAA breach vector — MFA for accounts containing PHI is now a baseline expectation despite not being expressly mandated by the Security Rule's 2003 baseline.

Warby Parker was published as a CMP rather than a settlement — meaning OCR proceeded to penalty imposition rather than negotiating a resolution agreement. Entities that do not contest the proposed determination effectively accept the CMP.
Health-adjacent retail (eyewear, hearing aids, dental) often forgets HIPAA covered-entity status applies. Marketing teams should not assume non-HIPAA status.

actions.case_name: In re Inmediata Health Group, Corp. (HHS OCR Resolution Agreement)
actions.full_caption: Resolution Agreement and Corrective Action Plan, Inmediata Health Group, Corp. (HHS Office for Civil Rights, Dec. 10, 2024)
statutes: HIPAA Security Rule (45 C.F.R. Part 164, Subpart C)
HIPAA Breach Notification Rule (45 C.F.R. Part 164, Subpart D)
actions.factual_pattern: December 10, 2024 OCR Resolution Agreement. Inmediata is a HIPAA business associate providing healthcare clearinghouse services. From May 16, 2016 through January 23, 2019, a misconfigured server (coding error in Inmediata's website) exposed the PHI of approximately 1,565,338 individuals on the open internet, where it was indexed and cached by search engines. Compromised PHI included names, dates of birth, addresses, Social Security numbers, claims information, and diagnosis/treatment information. OCR opened its investigation in 2018 after receiving a complaint and found multiple potential HIPAA Security Rule violations including failure to conduct a compliant risk analysis and failure to monitor information system activity. Settlement: $250,000 to OCR with no separate corrective action plan, because OCR was satisfied that the corrective measures imposed by the parallel 2023 multistate AG settlement (see hipaa-inmediata-multistate-2023) adequately addressed the noncompliance.
remediation_imposed: OCR settlement: $250,000 monetary payment
No separate OCR corrective action plan (deferred to existing 2023 multistate AG corrective action plan)
actions.citation: OCR Press Release and Resolution Agreement, Dec. 10, 2024.
operational_lessons: HIPAA business associates face direct OCR enforcement for breaches caused by misconfigurations and inadequate code review.

Configuration errors (cloud storage, public-facing servers, unsecured search-engine-indexed content) are a recurring breach vector for healthcare processors. AWS S3 / Azure Blob / GCP Storage configurations require specific HIPAA-aware deployment patterns.
OCR may decline to impose a separate corrective action plan when a parallel multistate AG settlement already mandates equivalent or stronger remediation — but the OCR monetary penalty is independent.

Healthcare clearinghouses, EHR vendors, billing services, and other healthcare BAs face cumulative monetary exposure across HIPAA OCR, multistate AG, and (where applicable) state comprehensive privacy law.

actions.case_name: In re Inmediata Health Group, Corp. — Multistate AG Settlement
actions.full_caption: Multistate AG Settlement with Inmediata Health Group, Corp. (32 States + Puerto Rico) (Oct. 17, 2023)
statutes: HIPAA Security Rule (45 C.F.R. Part 164, Subpart C)
HIPAA Breach Notification Rule (45 C.F.R. Part 164, Subpart D)
Various state consumer-protection and breach-notification statutes
actions.factual_pattern: October 17, 2023 multistate settlement led by Indiana AG Todd Rokita with Executive Committee of Connecticut, Michigan, and Tennessee, joined by 28 additional states plus Puerto Rico — 33 jurisdictions total. The settlement resolves allegations stemming from Inmediata's 2016-2019 web-exposure breach of 1,565,338 individuals' PHI. AGs alleged Inmediata violated state consumer protection laws, breach notification laws, and HIPAA by (1) failing to conduct any secure code review prior to the breach; (2) delaying breach notification by more than three months after OCR alerted Inmediata on January 15, 2019 (notification not issued until April 24, 2019, exceeding the 60-day HIPAA Breach Notification Rule window); and (3) sending misaddressed and inadequately detailed notification letters that failed to explain why Inmediata held recipients' data. Settlement: $1.4 million to states, plus comprehensive corrective action plan including code review controls, search-engine-crawling controls, incident response plan with specific notification policies, and five years of independent third-party security assessments.
remediation_imposed: Comprehensive information security program
Code review and crawling controls
Incident response plan with specific consumer-notification policies
Annual independent third-party security assessments for five years
Improved breach notification processes
actions.citation: Indiana AG Press Release Oct. 17, 2023; Multistate Settlement Agreement.
operational_lessons: Multistate AG actions in the healthcare-processor space can multiply OCR penalties by 5-6x — Inmediata's combined exposure was $1.65M (multistate $1.4M + OCR $250k).

Breach notification timing is independently enforceable under state breach notification statutes even when HIPAA's 60-day clock has run; AGs scrutinize notice content (clarity, why-we-have-your-data context) in addition to timeliness.
Lack of any prior secure code review is treated as an aggravating factor — security testing must be documented to demonstrate reasonable safeguards.

Healthcare clearinghouses and other BAs face cumulative exposure across HIPAA OCR, multistate AG, and (where applicable) state comprehensive privacy law — these regimes operate in parallel, not in lieu of one another.

actions.case_name: OCR Civil Money Penalty against Children's Hospital Colorado
actions.full_caption: OCR Notice of Proposed Determination — Children's Hospital Colorado
actions.factual_pattern: December 5, 2024 OCR civil money penalty. CHC suffered two cyberattacks (2017 and 2020) affecting 14,000+ pediatric patients. The 2017 breach: helpdesk turned off a physician's MFA without reactivating it; threat actor compromised the email account containing PHI of 3,370 children. The 2020 breach: workforce members allowed unauthorized third-party access to three accounts. OCR imposed $548,265 CMP comprising: $100k for failure to provide training; $100k for impermissible disclosure; $348,265 for risk-analysis failures (3 years × $100k + 35 days × $1,379/day continuing violation calculation). CHC waived hearing.
remediation_imposed: CMP imposed; CHC waived hearing rights
actions.citation: OCR Notice of Proposed Determination Dec 5, 2024.
operational_lessons: MFA disablement by helpdesk for a covered-entity workforce member is a per-se HIPAA Security Rule violation when not promptly re-enabled. Operational helpdesk procedures must include MFA re-activation as a hard requirement.

Risk analysis violations now calculated by OCR on a continuing-violation per-day basis (with $1,379/day for tier 1 violations as of 2024 inflation adjustment) — exposure compounds for multi-year program failures.
Pediatric covered entities face heightened scrutiny given child-data sensitivity. Combined with state minor-protection statutes (FL, NJ, OR, etc.) and COPPA where applicable.
Waiving the hearing is a strategic choice that makes the CMP final without admission of liability but also without contest of OCR's findings.

actions.case_name: Third Circuit CMIA / CIPA Dismissal (Pixel Direct-Recipient Theory)
actions.full_caption: Third Circuit affirmance of dismissal of CIPA / CMIA pixel claims (2025)
statutes: Cal. Civ. Code § 56 et seq. (Confidentiality of Medical Information Act / CMIA)
actions.factual_pattern: Third Circuit affirmed dismissal of CIPA and CMIA claims in pixel-tracking class action. CIPA holding: third-party pixel provider (Meta) was a direct recipient of plaintiff's browser communication, not an illegal interceptor — direct-recipient theory forecloses § 631 'aiding and abetting' liability. CMIA holding: rejected because plaintiff did not adequately allege CMIA-required elements. Combined with Solomon v. Flipps (2d Cir. VPPA), Thomas v. Papa John's (9th Cir. CIPA direct-party), this represents an emerging appellate consensus narrowing private wiretap/pixel theories.
remediation_imposed: Defense affirmance
actions.citation: Third Circuit ruling 2025 (case-specific citation).
operational_lessons: The 'direct recipient' defense (pixel provider receives the user's outgoing communication directly, so it's not an unauthorized interception) is gaining traction at the appellate level.
CMIA exposure narrowed where plaintiff cannot show CMIA-specific elements; healthcare-adjacent entities that are not licensed providers may have CMIA defenses unavailable to formal covered entities.

Combined appellate rulings (Solomon, Thomas, this Third Circuit decision) substantially improve the defense posture for pixel deployments — but the underlying compliance picture has not changed because state AG enforcement (Healthline) and federal enforcement (Cerebral) remain available even where private wiretap claims fail.
Forum selection remains critical: 9th Cir., 2d Cir., 3d Cir. are increasingly defense-favorable; 6th Cir. (Salazar v. Paramount) and some 1st Cir. authority remain plaintiff-favorable.

actions.case_name: ADVISORY — States With No Published Comprehensive-Privacy-Law Enforcement (as of corpus 2.2)
actions.full_caption: ADVISORY ENTRY — Not an enforcement action. Maintained to prevent users from inferring that absence from the corpus implies absence of risk.
statutes: Md. Code Com. Law § 14-4601 et seq. (MODPA)
actions.factual_pattern: As of the date of this corpus, the following 14 comprehensive-privacy-law states have NOT issued public enforcement actions, settlements, or AG cure-letter aggregations sufficient to merit individual entries: Virginia (VCDPA, effective Jan 2023), Utah (UCPA, effective Dec 2023), Iowa (effective Jan 2025), Indiana (effective Jan 2026), Tennessee (TIPA, effective Jul 2025), Delaware (effective Jan 2025), New Jersey (effective Jan 2025), New Hampshire (effective Jan 2025), Nebraska (effective Jan 2025), Kentucky (effective Jan 2026), Rhode Island (effective Jan 2026), Maryland (MODPA, effective Oct 2025), Minnesota (effective Jul 2025), Montana (effective Oct 2024). This entry is included intentionally so that users of the corpus do not infer that absence of published enforcement means absence of compliance risk in these states. Multiple of these laws have come into effect within the past 12-18 months and AG offices are still building enforcement capacity. By analogy, these AGs are likely to follow the patterns established by CT (privacy-notice sweeps), OR (cure-letter campaigns), and TX (proactive investigation under DTPA-style theories).
remediation_imposed: N/A
actions.citation: Per-state statutes; AG offices' public enforcement records reviewed as of corpus version date.
operational_lessons: Absence of public enforcement does not mean absence of investigation. Several of these AGs have published cure letters or compliance reviews not catalogued individually here.

When advising clients on these jurisdictions, treat the most analogous published enforcement (typically CT or OR) as the operational benchmark; for sensitive-data and minor questions, treat MODPA's flat-ban regime as the binding constraint where MD applies.

Maryland MODPA is the highest-stakes state-without-published-enforcement: its flat bans on sensitive-data sale and minor-data sale/targeted-advertising are the strictest substantive standards in any state law. First MODPA enforcement is anticipated and likely to focus on those bans.

Newly-effective laws (IN, KY, RI Jan 2026; TN Jul 2025; MN Jul 2025) follow predictable patterns: AG offices typically pursue obvious-failure cases first (no privacy notice; broken opt-out mechanisms) before reaching nuanced theories. Multi-state controllers should prioritize remediation of obvious failures across all applicable jurisdictions.
Update this entry as states publish enforcement; do not infer safe harbor from current absence.

Virginia is the longest-effective comprehensive law without published AG enforcement (effective Jan 2023). The absence is itself notable — VA AG has been substantially less public than CT despite similar law length-of-effective-time.

actions.case_name: U.S. v. The Walt Disney Company (COPPA / Made-for-Kids YouTube labeling)
actions.full_caption: United States v. Disney Worldwide Servs., Inc. and Disney Entm't Operations LLC (C.D. Cal. 2025)
actions.respondent: Disney Worldwide Services, Inc.; Disney Entertainment Operations LLC
statutes: Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (15 U.S.C. § 6501 et seq.)
actions.factual_pattern: September 2025 complaint; December 31, 2025 federal court approval. Following YouTube's 2019 COPPA settlement requiring content creators to designate uploads as 'Made for Kids' (MFK) or 'Not Made for Kids' (NMFK), Disney maintained a channel-level designation policy rather than reviewing video-by-video. FTC alleged this caused child-directed videos (including content from Frozen, Inside Out, Finding Dory, Encanto) to be incorrectly labeled NMFK on certain channels, enabling YouTube to collect personal data from child viewers and target advertising to them. The Pixar/Pixar Cars channel disparity (Pixar marked NMFK; Pixar Cars marked MFK for similar content) was cited as illustrative.
remediation_imposed: $10M civil penalty
Permanent injunction barring Disney from operating on YouTube in violation of COPPA
10-year 'Audience Designation Program' requiring video-by-video MFK review (unless YouTube implements age-assurance technologies or removes the MFK labeling feature)
Compliance reporting
actions.citation: FTC Press Release Sept 2025; Court Approval Order Dec 31, 2025; DOJ Press Release.
operational_lessons: COPPA enforcement extends beyond direct child-data collection to platform-mediated collection where the content creator is responsible for accurate audience designation.
Channel-level designation policies are inadequate where individual videos vary in audience direction. Content creators must implement video-by-video review.

10-year audience-designation programs are an emerging structural remedy — like FTC's 20-year orders for Cerebral and GM, the compounding compliance cost over the order's term substantially exceeds the headline civil penalty.

Companies producing child-directed content for distribution on third-party platforms (YouTube, TikTok, Roblox, etc.) face COPPA exposure for the platform's data collection practices, not only their own.
Combined with Tilting Point ($500k Cal. AG, 2024) and NGL Labs ($5M FTC + Cal. AG, 2024), the federal-state enforcement trend in child privacy is intensifying.

actions.case_name: In re Equifax Information Services LLC (CFPB)
actions.full_caption: In the Matter of Equifax Information Services LLC, CFPB Consent Order
statutes: Fair Credit Reporting Act (15 U.S.C. § 1681 et seq.)
actions.factual_pattern: January 2025 CFPB consent order. CFPB alleged that Equifax failed to follow reasonable procedures to ensure maximum possible accuracy of consumer reports, mishandled consumer disputes (insufficient investigation, deletion of dispute documentation, automated dispute outcomes contradicting investigator findings), and violated FCRA dispute-handling timelines. Settlement: $15 million civil penalty plus mandated dispute-handling reforms.
remediation_imposed: Comprehensive overhaul of dispute investigation procedures
Mandated retention of dispute documentation
Independent monitor for compliance
Three years of compliance reporting
actions.citation: CFPB Press Release January 2025.
operational_lessons: CRA dispute-handling is enforced against accuracy AND process — CFPB scrutinizes whether the actual dispute investigation matches what is reported back to the consumer.
Automated dispute outcomes that contradict investigator findings are an enforcement target — algorithmic-decision review must align with documented investigator conclusions.
FCRA's dispute timelines (30/45 days under § 1681i(a)) are strict-liability when missed, separate from the accuracy of the underlying determination.
Regulated professionals are themselves consumers in disputes with CRAs — controllers should understand their employees' and clients' rights as a parallel compliance dimension.

actions.case_name: Healthcare Sector Pixel Class-Action Settlement Pattern (Hospital Systems, 2024-2025)
actions.full_caption: Various — Advocate Aurora ($12.225M); Novant Health ($6.6M); UCSF/Dignity Health ($12.25M); WakeMed; others
actions.respondent: Multiple — Advocate Aurora Health, Novant Health, UCSF Medical Center, Dignity Health, WakeMed, others
statutes: 18 U.S.C. § 2510 et seq. (ECPA / Federal Wiretap Act)
actions.factual_pattern: Following the Doe v. Meta MDL framework, hundreds of healthcare-pixel class actions have settled in 2023-2025 for amounts ranging from low seven figures to mid eight figures. Representative settled matters: Advocate Aurora Health (~$12.225M, 3M class members; Meta Pixel on patient portal); Novant Health ($6.6M, 1.36M class members); UCSF/Dignity Health ($12.25M); WakeMed Health & Hospitals (~$8M). Common fact pattern: Meta Pixel deployed on hospital patient portals or scheduling pages transmitted patient PHI (URL paths revealing condition or specialty, appointment requests, message content) to Meta in real time. AHA v. Becerra (June 2024) narrowed OCR's authority but did not eliminate private-action exposure.
remediation_imposed: Settlement funds (varying by class size)
Pixel removal from authenticated patient portals
Notice to affected patients
Some matters: limited future use of pixels with affirmative consent
actions.citation: Various district court filings 2023-2025; settlement orders.
operational_lessons: Hospital-pixel class actions are among the highest-volume privacy litigation areas in 2024-2025. Combined private-action exposure across the sector likely exceeds $100M annually.
Settlement valuations cluster around $4-10/class member, with sensitive-condition signals driving higher valuations.
AHA v. Becerra (vacating OCR's Bulletin) reduces HHS exposure but does not affect private CIPA, ECPA, state-CMIA, or state-comprehensive-privacy-law theories that continue to drive these settlements.

Removal of third-party pixels from authenticated patient portals is the single highest-leverage compliance action for healthcare providers. This should be a Day-1 priority for any healthcare-adjacent compliance engagement.
Healthcare BAs (clearinghouses, EHR vendors, scheduling vendors) face derivative exposure when their products embed third-party trackers that fire on covered-entity-customer pages.

actions.case_name: Healthcare-Adjacent Class Action — Salinas v. Block (Cash App data breach class)
actions.full_caption: Salinas v. Block, Inc., No. 3:22-cv-04823 (N.D. Cal.)
statutes: Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.150 (CCPA private right of action)
actions.factual_pattern: Class settlement for $15 million approved 2024-2025 (payments ongoing since April 2025) for the December 2021 Cash App data breach affecting approximately 8.2 million users. Insider exposure: a former Cash App Investing employee accessed customer reports without authorization. CCPA § 1798.150 private right of action invoked for breach of duty to maintain reasonable security. Companion to the CFPB consent order ($175M) and 48-state-regulator settlement ($80M) — total Block exposure across all actions exceeds $295M.
remediation_imposed: Class fund distribution
Mandated security program enhancements (overlapping with CFPB consent order)
actions.citation: Salinas v. Block, Inc., No. 3:22-cv-04823 (N.D. Cal.); settlement approved 2024.
operational_lessons: CCPA's § 1798.150 private right of action is the rare PRA in state comprehensive privacy law and is plaintiff-bar's primary tool for class actions following data breaches affecting California residents.
Insider-access breaches face the same exposure as external attacks — controllers must implement access controls and offboarding procedures that prevent post-employment access.
Cumulative exposure model: a single security incident can produce CFPB ($175M), state-regulator ($80M), private CCPA class action ($15M), and other state-AG actions, all in parallel.

Fintech and consumer-finance entities face the most pronounced layered exposure of any industry: federal financial regulator (CFPB, OCC, FRB), state financial regulator, state AGs, FTC for non-bank financial entities, plus private class actions.

actions.case_name: People v. Disney (streaming opt-out failures)
actions.full_caption: People v. Disney Streaming Services, Inc. et al. (Cal. Super. Ct., Feb. 2026)
actions.respondent: Disney Streaming Services, Inc. and related Disney entities
actions.factual_pattern: February 2026 settlement — the largest CCPA enforcement settlement in California history as of that date. Following the AG's 2024 investigative sweep of streaming services, the AG alleged that Disney's streaming ecosystem (Disney+, Hulu, ESPN+) failed to honor opt-out requests across all devices and services associated with consumer accounts. Consumers who opted out through one service or device were not opted out across their full account. The AG alleged this constituted a systematic failure to honor the right to opt out of sale and sharing under CCPA.
remediation_imposed: Largest CCPA settlement in California history as of Feb. 2026
Account-wide opt-out processing required — opt-out on one device/service must propagate across all associated devices and services
GPC must be honored across streaming platforms
Enhanced privacy notice requirements
Multi-year compliance reporting to AG
Risk assessments under 2026 CCPA regulations
actions.citation: CA AG Press Release Feb. 11, 2026; Stipulated Final Judgment.
operational_lessons: Opt-out rights must be honored account-wide, not device-by-device or service-by-service. A consumer's opt-out propagates across all products and services associated with their account.
Streaming and multi-platform services face heightened scrutiny under the AG's 2024 investigative sweep — the second public CA streaming settlement in 12 months.
The AG will compound enforcement leverage using UCL § 17200 alongside CCPA, which provides independent remedial authority.
Dark pattern opt-out flows (making opt-out harder than opting in, or fragmenting it across devices) is a leading enforcement theory in CA in 2025-2026.
Settlement amounts are scaling with repeat enforcement signals — the AG has established a pattern of progressive escalation.

actions.case_name: In re Ford Motor Company (CPPA)
actions.full_caption: In the Matter of Ford Motor Company, CPPA Enforcement (2025)
actions.factual_pattern: CPPA enforcement action against Ford Motor Company as part of the agency's connected-vehicle investigative sweep announced in July 2023. Ford required consumers to provide excessive verification information to exercise opt-out rights and failed to implement opt-out mechanisms that were symmetrical with opt-in flows, in violation of CCPA Regs § 7060. Similar in pattern to the Honda enforcement action (ca-cppa-honda-2025) — both arising from the connected-vehicle sweep and both citing verification overreach and asymmetric consent mechanics.
remediation_imposed: Conform verification requirements to § 7060 (no excessive information for opt-out requests)
Implement symmetrical opt-in/opt-out flows
Compliance reporting to CPPA
actions.citation: CPPA Enforcement Action 2025 (connected-vehicle sweep); verify for final order.
operational_lessons: Connected-vehicle manufacturers are under systematic CPPA scrutiny. Honda and Ford are the first two public actions from the July 2023 sweep — others are expected.
Verification overreach for opt-out requests is a core CPPA enforcement priority under § 7060. The CPPA treats excessive verification as a de facto barrier to opt-out rights.
Asymmetric consent mechanics — where opting in requires fewer steps than opting out — violates dark-pattern regulations and is independently actionable.
CPPA enforcement is extending the connected-vehicle cases broadly — findings are explicitly stated to apply to any CCPA-covered business, not just automotive.
